META.subject: Biology
META.subtitleDoc: CBE Phenomenon-Driven Lesson Sequence — Sub-Strand 2.1 (10 Lessons)

UNIT.subject: Biology
UNIT.strand: Strand 2.0: Physiology of Plants and Animals
UNIT.content:
• Autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition in plants
• Structure and function of the leaf as an organ of photosynthesis
• Raw materials for photosynthesis: carbon dioxide, water, light energy, chlorophyll
• Light-dependent and light-independent (dark) reactions of photosynthesis
• Factors affecting the rate of photosynthesis: light intensity, carbon dioxide concentration, temperature, water availability
• Experiments to investigate factors affecting the rate of photosynthesis (e.g., DCPIP decolourisation, oxygen bubble counting in Elodea/aquatic plants)
• Leaf adaptations for photosynthesis (thin lamina, large surface area, stomata, chloroplasts, vascular bundles)
• Mineral nutrition in plants: macronutrients and micronutrients, deficiency symptoms
• Importance of photosynthesis to ecosystems and food chains (producer role)
• Applications of plant nutrition: greenhouse farming, hydroponics, fertiliser use in Kenyan agriculture
UNIT.learningOutcomes:
By the end of the sub-strand, the learner should be able to:
a) distinguish between autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition in plants,
b) describe the structure of the leaf and relate it to its role as the primary organ of photosynthesis,
c) explain the process of photosynthesis, including the light-dependent and light-independent reactions and the role of each raw material,
d) investigate and explain the factors that affect the rate of photosynthesis through experiments,
e) describe the role of mineral nutrients in plant growth and identify deficiency symptoms,
f) evaluate the significance of photosynthesis to ecosystems, agriculture, and food security in Kenya,
g) appreciate the importance of plant nutrition in sustaining life on Earth.
UNIT.coreCompetencies:
• Communication and Collaboration: Learners collaboratively design and carry out experiments on photosynthesis (e.g., counting oxygen bubbles from aquatic plants in a Kisumu lake-water setup) and present findings to peers using annotated diagrams and data tables.
• Critical Thinking and Problem Solving: Learners analyse experimental results on factors affecting photosynthesis, identify anomalies in data, and propose explanations for why sukuma wiki grown in a shaded kitchen garden produces smaller leaves than those grown in full sunlight.
• Imagination and Creativity: Learners construct annotated 2D or 3D models of a leaf cross-section using locally available materials (cardboard, cellophane, drinking straws) to illustrate adaptations for photosynthesis.
• Digital Literacy: Where devices are available, learners use simulations or videos to visualise the light-dependent and light-independent reactions and record observations in digital or written logs.
• Self-efficacy: Learners develop confidence in scientific inquiry by designing fair-test investigations into one factor affecting the rate of photosynthesis and evaluating the reliability of their own results.
UNIT.values:
• Responsibility: Learners handle laboratory chemicals (iodine solution, ethanol) and living specimens (leaves, aquatic plants) safely and dispose of waste appropriately, reflecting responsible stewardship of school resources and the environment.
• Respect: Learners listen to and value peers' experimental observations and alternative explanations during group sensemaking discussions, appreciating diverse scientific thinking.
• Unity: Learners work in mixed-ability groups during practical investigations, sharing roles equitably and recognising that collaborative effort produces more reliable scientific conclusions.
• Social Justice: Learners connect plant nutrition to food security in Kenya, discussing how understanding photosynthesis and soil mineral nutrition can help smallholder farmers in the Rift Valley and Western Kenya improve crop yields.
UNIT.sep:
• Asking Questions and Defining Problems: Learners generate investigable questions from the anchoring phenomenon (e.g., "Why does the sukuma wiki wilt in the midday sun?") and refine them into testable hypotheses about water availability and photosynthesis rate.
• Planning and Carrying Out Investigations: Learners plan and conduct fair-test experiments — including the variegated leaf starch test and the aquatic-plant oxygen-bubble experiment — controlling variables and selecting appropriate equipment.
• Analysing and Interpreting Data: Learners tabulate, graph, and interpret data from photosynthesis rate experiments, identifying trends and explaining anomalous results using scientific reasoning.
• Constructing Explanations: Learners build written and oral explanations linking leaf structure, raw materials, and environmental conditions to observed rates of photosynthesis, returning to the anchoring phenomenon at each stage.
• Developing and Using Models: Learners iteratively revise a class model of the leaf and the photosynthesis process on the Driving Question Board, adding new mechanism layers after each lesson.
• Obtaining, Evaluating, and Communicating Information: Learners read secondary sources on Kenyan agricultural research (e.g., KALRO recommendations on fertiliser use) and evaluate the evidence for mineral nutrient deficiency symptoms in maize and beans.
UNIT.pcis:
• Environmental Conservation: Learners examine how deforestation in the Mau Forest reduces the photosynthetic capacity of ecosystems and discuss the consequences for carbon cycling, rainfall patterns, and local food production.
• Food and Nutrition Education: Learners connect the productivity of photosynthesis in staple crops (maize, beans, sukuma wiki) to Kenya's national food security goals, linking plant biology to human dietary needs.
• Agriculture and Food Security: Learners investigate how the use of fertilisers supplying macronutrients (N, P, K) and micronutrients on small-scale farms in the Rift Valley can correct mineral deficiencies and increase crop photosynthetic output.
• Safety and Security: Learners follow laboratory safety protocols when using ethanol (flammable) during the starch test for photosynthesis experiment and when handling iodine solution, demonstrating responsible laboratory behaviour.
• Financial Literacy: Learners evaluate the cost-benefit of using inorganic fertilisers versus organic compost for a maize farmer in Nakuru, connecting understanding of mineral nutrition to practical economic decision-making.
UNIT.careers:
• Botanist: This sub-strand provides foundational knowledge of plant physiology and photosynthesis that underpins degree-level botanical research, including work at institutions such as the National Museums of Kenya.
• Agricultural Scientist / Agronomist: Understanding mineral nutrition, photosynthesis, and factors limiting plant growth is directly applied by agronomists advising Kenyan smallholder farmers on fertiliser use and crop management with organisations like KALRO.
• Horticulturist / Greenhouse Farmer: Knowledge of optimising light intensity, CO₂ concentration, and mineral nutrition is essential for running commercial greenhouse operations (e.g., flower and vegetable farms in Naivasha and Thika).
• Environmental Scientist: Understanding photosynthesis as the engine of primary production and carbon fixation connects to careers in climate science, carbon sequestration, and ecosystem monitoring in Kenya's forests and wetlands.
• Biochemist / Biotechnologist: The light-dependent and light-independent reactions introduce metabolic pathways (ATP synthesis, Calvin cycle) that form the basis of biochemistry and biotechnology careers, including development of drought-resistant crops at KARI/KALRO.
• Secondary School Biology Teacher: Mastery of plant nutrition and photosynthesis prepares learners to eventually communicate these concepts as educators, supporting Kenya's Vision 2030 science education goals.
UNIT.focus: This unit anchors learning in a locally familiar, puzzling observation — sukuma wiki and spinach leaves wilting in the hot midday sun in a Nairobi kitchen garden — and uses it to drive a 10-lesson investigation into how plants make their own food through photosynthesis and how they obtain the mineral nutrients needed to grow. Learners move from initial predictions about why plants wilt and why leaves are green, through hands-on experiments and data analysis, toward a mechanistic understanding of photosynthesis (including light-dependent and light-independent reactions), the role of leaf structure, and the importance of mineral nutrition. Throughout the unit, every new concept is connected back to the anchoring phenomenon and to the real-world context of Kenyan agriculture, food security, and environmental conservation.
UNIT.phenomenon: A form 4 student in Nairobi notices that the sukuma wiki (kale) plants in her family's kitchen garden look healthy and upright in the early morning, but by midday — when the sun is strongest — the leaves have wilted visibly, even though the soil appears moist. The following morning they have recovered. The student also notices that when the plants are moved under the shade of a mango tree, the wilting stops but the leaves gradually become pale yellowish-green over several weeks and the plant grows more slowly. This phenomenon is puzzling because: (1) wilting happens even when water seems available in the soil, suggesting something about the rate of water movement or use is changing; (2) shade prevents wilting but causes a new problem — pallor and slow growth — suggesting that light is essential for something the plant needs to do; and (3) the plant recovers overnight, implying a reversible process rather than permanent damage. These observations create genuine puzzlement that motivates questions about how plants get energy, what role light plays, why leaves are green, and what the plant needs from the soil and air to stay healthy and grow.
UNIT.supportingPhenomena:
• A maize farmer in Nakuru notices that one section of her shamba has stunted, yellow-leaved maize plants despite receiving the same rainfall as the healthy green section — prompting questions about what else plants need from their environment besides water and light (mineral nutrition / nitrogen deficiency).
• A student conducting a DCPIP decolourisation experiment in the school lab observes that the blue dye fades faster when a test tube of leaf extract is placed near a bright window than when kept in a dark cupboard — raising questions about the role of light energy in chemical reactions inside leaves.
• Aquatic plants (e.g., Elodea or locally available pond plants from Lake Victoria) visibly produce more oxygen bubbles per minute when placed under a bright lamp than under dim light or in the dark — providing a quantitative, observable proxy for the rate of photosynthesis.
• Variegated leaves (green and white sections) of a commonly available ornamental plant, when tested for starch with iodine solution after a period in the light, show blue-black colouration only in the green sections — demonstrating that chlorophyll is required for photosynthesis to produce starch.
UNIT.drivingQuestion:
DRIVING QUESTION: Why does our sukuma wiki wilt in the midday sun even when the soil is wet — and what does a plant actually need to stay green, grow, and make its own food?

KICD KEY INQUIRY QUESTIONS:
1. How do plants make their own food?
2. What factors affect the rate at which plants make food?
UNIT.storylineThread:
Lesson 1 — ANCHORING PHENOMENON & PREDICT: Students observe photographs and a short description of the wilting sukuma wiki phenomenon. They write individual predictions about why it wilts and why shade causes pallor, and contribute opening questions to the class Driving Question Board (DQB). The teacher introduces autotrophic vs. heterotrophic nutrition to frame the unit question.

Lesson 2 — LEAF STRUCTURE & OBSERVE: Students dissect or examine prepared cross-sections of a dicot leaf (e.g., from a locally available plant such as a mango or bean leaf) under a hand lens or microscope. They identify and annotate the epidermis, palisade mesophyll, spongy mesophyll, stomata, guard cells, and vascular bundles, linking each feature to its function in photosynthesis and water movement — beginning to explain the wilting phenomenon.

Lesson 3 — RAW MATERIALS & EXPLAIN: Through a structured reading and guided discussion, learners identify the four raw materials for photosynthesis (CO₂, H₂O, light energy, chlorophyll) and write the word equation and chemical equation. They revisit the DQB: which raw materials could explain wilting (water/light) and pallor (light/chlorophyll)?

Lesson 4 — STARCH TEST PRACTICAL (OBSERVE): Learners carry out the standard starch test on a variegated leaf (or a leaf that has been partially covered with black paper) using ethanol decolourisation and iodine solution. They observe that only the green, illuminated sections turn blue-black, providing evidence that chlorophyll and light are both required for photosynthesis to produce starch. DQB is updated.

Lesson 5 — LIGHT-DEPENDENT REACTIONS (EXPLAIN): Using a diagram and guided notes, learners trace the light-dependent reactions: absorption of light by chlorophyll, photolysis of water, production of ATP and NADPH, and release of oxygen. They connect this to the oxygen bubbles observed in the supporting phenomenon and to the role of light in the sukuma wiki phenomenon.

Lesson 6 — LIGHT-INDEPENDENT REACTIONS / CALVIN CYCLE (EXPLAIN): Learners explore the light-independent reactions: fixation of CO₂ using ATP and NADPH from the light stage, production of glucose (G3P). They construct a simplified flow diagram linking both stages and return to the DQB to explain why shaded sukuma wiki grows slowly (less ATP and glucose produced).

Lesson 7 — FACTORS AFFECTING RATE OF PHOTOSYNTHESIS — INVESTIGATION (PLAN & OBSERVE): Learners plan and carry out a quantitative investigation using an aquatic plant (or a simulated dataset if live plants are unavailable), counting oxygen bubbles per minute at different light intensities (covering the lamp with layers of muslin or changing distance). They record data in tables and plot line graphs, identifying the limiting factor concept.

Lesson 8 — FACTORS AFFECTING RATE — DATA ANALYSIS & EXPLAIN: Learners analyse their graphs from Lesson 7 and examine provided datasets for the effects of CO₂ concentration and temperature on photosynthesis rate. They apply the concept of limiting factors to explain real agricultural scenarios (e.g., why greenhouse tomato farmers in Naivasha add CO₂ to boost yields).

Lesson 9 — MINERAL NUTRITION (OBSERVE & EXPLAIN): Using photographs and secondary data from KALRO research, learners identify macronutrients (N, P, K, Ca, Mg, S) and micronutrients, describe their roles in plant growth, and match deficiency symptoms to specific minerals (e.g., yellowing of older leaves = nitrogen deficiency in the Nakuru maize farmer supporting phenomenon). They connect mineral uptake via roots to the overall nutrition of the sukuma wiki plant.

Lesson 10 — MODEL BUILDING, SYNTHESIS & DQB RESOLUTION: Learners produce a final annotated model (poster or digital diagram) that integrates leaf structure, the two stages of photosynthesis, limiting factors, and mineral nutrition to fully explain the original anchoring phenomenon (why sukuma wiki wilts at midday and turns pale in shade). Groups present their models, the class resolves all remaining DQB questions, and learners write a final evidence-based explanation connecting plant nutrition to Kenyan agriculture and food security.

LESSONS.title: Why Does Our Sukuma Wiki Wilt? Anchoring the Phenomenon and Opening Our Questions
LESSONS.aresKeywords: autotrophic nutrition, heterotrophic nutrition, photosynthesis, wilting, chlorophyll, phenomenon, prediction

slo.purpose: To anchor learners in a puzzling, familiar Kenyan agricultural phenomenon that will drive inquiry across the entire sub-strand on plant nutrition
slo.knowledge: Learners will distinguish between autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition and state that green plants make their own food using light energy
slo.skills: Learners will record observations, write evidence-based predictions, and generate investigable questions about the sukuma wiki phenomenon
slo.attitudes: Learners will demonstrate curiosity and open-mindedness by sharing predictions without fear of being wrong, and appreciate that everyday observations in Kenyan homes and gardens can be the starting point for scientific inquiry
slo.keyInquiry: How do plants make their own food?
slo.purposeInStoryline: This is the opening anchoring lesson. It launches the phenomenon that every subsequent lesson will return to and explain more fully. By the end of this lesson learners have a shared puzzlement, a public record of their initial ideas, and a Driving Question Board that will grow across all 10 lessons until it is fully resolved in Lesson 10
slo.safety1otes: No chemicals or sharp instruments are used in this lesson. If printed photographs are handled, ensure edges are not sharp. If learners are asked to bring a sukuma wiki leaf from home, remind them to wash hands after handling any plant material.

LESSONS.overview: Learners are introduced to the central anchoring phenomenon: a Nairobi student observes that her family sukuma wiki wilts at midday despite moist soil, recovers overnight, but turns pale and grows slowly when moved into shade. Through individual written predictions, a think-pair-share discussion, and a class Driving Question Board, learners surface prior knowledge and genuine puzzlement. The teacher then introduces the key distinction between autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition to frame the unit. The lesson closes with learners identifying at least two questions they personally want answered, linking to the driving question that will guide the whole sub-strand.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are shown two high-quality photographs displayed on the board or printed on A4 sheets: Photograph A shows a healthy upright sukuma wiki plant in the early morning in a Nairobi kitchen garden, and Photograph B shows the same patch of sukuma wiki visibly wilted at midday with the soil appearing dark and moist. A third image shows the sukuma wiki after being moved under a mango tree: the leaves are now pale yellowish-green and the plant looks stunted. The teacher reads aloud a short narrative about Amina, a Form 4 student from Kasarani, Nairobi, who observes all three of these changes in her family garden. Learners are given 4 minutes of silent individual writing time to respond to three prompt questions in their notebooks: (1) Why do you think the sukuma wiki wilts at midday even though the soil is wet? (2) Why do you think it recovers by the next morning? (3) Why do you think moving it into shade causes the leaves to turn pale and the plant to grow slowly? Learners are explicitly told that there are no wrong answers at this stage and that their predictions will be revisited and refined throughout the unit.
framework.teacherMoves: The teacher displays the photographs and says: 'Look carefully at these two images of sukuma wiki — a plant most of you have eaten or seen growing at home. I want you to notice something puzzling. In the morning the plants look strong and green. By midday, with the soil still wet, the leaves are drooping. And when Amina moved the plants into the shade to stop the wilting, something new went wrong. Before we learn anything new, I want to know what YOU think is happening. Write your predictions in your notebook — full sentences please. I am not looking for the right answer. I am looking for YOUR thinking.' The teacher then circulates silently for 4 minutes, reading over shoulders without commenting, noting interesting predictions to return to later. WAIT TIME: Allow a full 4 minutes of silent individual writing before moving to pairs. Do not prompt or hint during this time.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Individual written prediction activates prior knowledge and creates a baseline record of each learner's initial conceptual model. Photographs ground the phenomenon in a familiar Kenyan context, reducing the cognitive distance between science class and real life.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher reads over shoulders during silent writing to identify the range of prior ideas: do learners link wilting to water? Do any mention sunlight or temperature? Do any mention food-making? These observations inform which student ideas to amplify in the discussion. Teacher mentally notes two or three predictions that represent contrasting ideas to share with the class.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners share their predictions first in pairs for 2 minutes, then the teacher takes 4-5 predictions from different pairs and writes them verbatim on the board under the heading 'Our Current Thinking.' The teacher does not evaluate any prediction as right or wrong. Predictions might include: 'The sun heats the water and it dries up,' 'The plant uses water faster than the roots can take it in,' 'Shade is cold so the plant cannot make food,' or 'The plant needs sunlight to stay green.' After recording predictions, the teacher displays a third image: a photograph of a mushroom growing on a log next to a photograph of a succulent green maize plant growing in a Rift Valley farm. The teacher asks learners to observe and discuss in pairs: 'What is the most important difference between how a mushroom gets its food and how a maize plant gets its food?' Pairs share responses. The teacher collects the key idea that the maize plant makes its own food while the mushroom depends on other organic matter, but does not yet formally define the terms.
framework.teacherMoves: After pairs have discussed, the teacher asks: 'Who heard something from their partner that was different from their own prediction — something that made you think?' Teacher calls on 3 learners to share. Then the teacher says: 'Look at these two organisms. Both are alive. Both need energy. But notice — the maize is out in the open sun, making something for itself. The mushroom is tucked under a log, getting energy from something that was once alive. What does that difference tell us about how they survive?' WAIT TIME: After posing the maize-mushroom question, wait 30 seconds in silence before taking responses. Allow learners to sit with the question. Do not rephrase or simplify it.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Paired discussion lowers the affective barrier to sharing ideas publicly. The maize-mushroom contrast is a locally recognisable juxtaposition that sets up the autotroph-heterotroph distinction without using the terms yet, allowing learners to construct the concept before receiving the vocabulary.
framework.formativeAssessment: Listen to pair discussions: can learners articulate that the green plant uses sunlight to make food while the mushroom cannot? This reveals whether learners hold a naive view that plants eat from the soil the way animals eat food, which is a known misconception to address in Lesson 3.

framework.learnerExperience: The teacher introduces the formal vocabulary of autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition. Learners copy a structured two-column comparison into their notebooks: Autotrophic Nutrition (auto = self, trophe = feeding) — the organism manufactures its own organic food from simple inorganic raw materials using an external energy source such as light; examples include all green plants, algae, and some bacteria. Heterotrophic Nutrition — the organism cannot manufacture its own food and must consume ready-made organic molecules from other organisms; examples include all animals, fungi, and most bacteria. The teacher connects this directly to the phenomenon: 'Amina noticed that her sukuma wiki wilted and turned pale. If the sukuma wiki plant is autotrophic — if it makes its own food — what must it need to do that? And what happens to the plant if it cannot make its food properly?' Learners write one sentence in their notebooks completing this stem: 'If sukuma wiki is autotrophic, it must need __________ to make food, and if it cannot do this, the plant will __________.' The teacher takes 4-5 responses verbatim and writes them on the board, noting that the class will spend the next several lessons finding out exactly which raw materials are needed and what each one does.
framework.teacherMoves: The teacher writes AUTOTROPHIC and HETEROTROPHIC on the board, breaking down the Greek roots. Then says: 'These are not just vocabulary words. These words tell us something important about the sukuma wiki problem. If Amina is going to figure out why her plant is struggling, she needs to understand what it means for a plant to feed itself. Let us think about what that might require.' After learners write their sentence completions, the teacher reads two or three aloud and says: 'Hold onto these ideas. We are going to test them, challenge them, and improve them over the next few weeks. By Lesson 10 you will be able to answer this question with real evidence.' WAIT TIME: After asking learners to complete the sentence stem, give 2 full minutes of silent writing time before taking responses.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Introducing autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition as a conceptual frame — rather than as isolated definitions — gives learners a lens through which to view all subsequent content. Connecting the terms immediately back to the sukuma wiki phenomenon prevents vocabulary from becoming inert knowledge.
framework.formativeAssessment: Read the sentence completions. Key things to look for: do learners identify light as a requirement? Do any mention water, soil, or air? Do any connect failure to make food to wilting or pallor? These will confirm which raw materials are already in learners mental models, informing the emphasis needed in Lesson 3.

framework.learnerExperience: Each learner receives two sticky notes (or cuts two small squares from scrap paper if sticky notes are unavailable). On the first note, they write one question they have about why the sukuma wiki wilts or turns pale — a question they genuinely do not yet know the answer to. On the second note, they write one question they have about how plants make food in general. Learners bring their notes to a large sheet of manila paper or a section of the classroom wall designated as the Driving Question Board. The teacher reads the driving question aloud from the top of the board: 'Why does our sukuma wiki wilt in the midday sun even when the soil is wet — and what does a plant actually need to stay green, grow, and make its own food?' Learners stick their questions below the driving question. The teacher then reads selected questions aloud and works with the class to group them loosely into emerging clusters: questions about water and wilting, questions about light and colour, questions about food-making, and questions about what plants take from soil and air. The clusters are labelled with card strips. Learners are told that the DQB will be revisited and updated after every lesson.
framework.teacherMoves: The teacher says: 'This board belongs to you. Every question up here was written by someone in this room. Over the next ten lessons we are going to answer these questions one by one using evidence — from experiments, from data, from what scientists in Kenya and around the world have discovered. At the end of the unit, every question on this board should have an answer — an answer YOU can explain.' The teacher reads 5-6 questions aloud, placing them on the board and asking the class: 'Does this question fit with these others about water, or does it belong with the questions about light?' to demonstrate the clustering process. Then learners help place the remaining questions. WAIT TIME: After asking 'What question are you most curious about from today?' pause 20 seconds before taking responses. Honour whatever question a learner shares, even if it goes beyond the syllabus.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The DQB makes learner curiosity visible and public, creating shared intellectual ownership of the inquiry. Grouping questions by theme previews the structure of the sub-strand and helps learners see that their genuine questions align with the key inquiry questions of the curriculum.
framework.formativeAssessment: The questions on the DQB are a rich formative record. Teacher photographs the board or writes a list of all questions. Look for: How many learners ask about water versus light versus soil? Are there questions that reveal misconceptions (e.g., do plants breathe like animals)? Are there questions that anticipate later lessons (e.g., what is chlorophyll)? Use this record to prioritise examples and emphasis in Lessons 2 through 10.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners spend the final 5 minutes drawing an initial model in their notebooks. The prompt is: 'Draw a simple sketch of Amina sukuma wiki plant and use arrows or labels to show what you currently think is going INTO the plant, what is HAPPENING INSIDE the plant, and what is COMING OUT of the plant. You can use words and pictures. This is your starting model — not your final answer.' Learners label their sketch with the date and write: 'My Starting Model — Lesson 1.' They are told they will add to, revise, and improve this model after each lesson, and that by Lesson 10 their final model will be detailed, accurate, and supported by evidence.
framework.teacherMoves: The teacher says: 'Scientists do not just write notes — they build models. A model is a way of showing how you think something works. Your model right now will probably be incomplete and some parts might be wrong — and that is exactly right for Lesson 1. I want to see YOUR thinking, not a textbook diagram. We will return to this model in every lesson and watch it grow.' Teacher circulates, asking individual learners: 'What made you choose that arrow?' or 'What do you think is happening inside the leaf?' to prompt elaboration without correcting. WAIT TIME: Allow the full 5 minutes without interruption. Resist the urge to show a correct model or textbook diagram at this stage.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The initial model externalises each learner existing conceptual model before instruction, creating a baseline for tracking conceptual change across the unit. The practice of revisiting and revising the model after each lesson builds metacognitive awareness of learning as a process of refinement rather than replacement.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect or photograph initial models to compare with Lesson 10 final models. Look for common starting points: do learners show soil going in (correct), sun shining on the plant (correct, partially), water being absorbed (correct), oxygen or food coming out (rare at this stage)? Note which learners already include light and which focus entirely on soil and water — this informs differentiation in Lesson 3.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, ask yourself: (1) Did learners engage with the phenomenon as genuinely puzzling, or did they seem to already know the answer? If the latter, probe more deeply in Lesson 2 about the mechanism, not just the outcome. (2) What misconceptions appeared in the predictions and DQB questions? Common ones include: plants eat from soil the way animals eat food, wilting is caused by heat drying the soil rather than by water movement within the plant, and shade is cold rather than low in light energy. Plan to address these explicitly in Lessons 3 and 5. (3) Were there learners who did not contribute to the DQB? Find a private moment before Lesson 2 to invite their questions individually. (4) Review the photograph of the DQB — are all four curriculum themes (water and wilting, light and colour, food-making processes, soil nutrients) represented in learner questions? If any cluster is thin, plan a teacher-posed question to seed it in Lesson 2. (5) Were predictions written in full sentences with reasoning? If not, reinforce the science writing expectation in the Lesson 2 notebook prompt.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: We observed photographs showing sukuma wiki wilting at midday despite moist soil, recovering overnight, and turning pale when moved into shade under a mango tree in a Nairobi kitchen garden
summaryTablePrompt.learned: We learned that green plants are autotrophs — they manufacture their own food from simple inorganic raw materials — while organisms like mushrooms are heterotrophs that depend on ready-made organic food from other sources
summaryTablePrompt.explained: We cannot yet fully explain why the sukuma wiki wilts at midday or why it turns pale in shade, but we have recorded our starting predictions and questions on the Driving Question Board and will return to them with evidence in every lesson that follows

LESSONS.title: Leaf Structure: The Architecture of a Photosynthetic Machine

slo.purpose: To examine the internal and external structure of a dicot leaf and link each structural feature to its function in photosynthesis and water movement, advancing the class explanation of the sukuma wiki wilting phenomenon.
slo.knowledge: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) identify and label the structural features of a dicot leaf (epidermis, cuticle, palisade mesophyll, spongy mesophyll, stomata, guard cells, vascular bundles); (b) explain how each feature is adapted to its function in gas exchange, water transport, and light capture; (c) relate leaf structural features to the wilting and recovery pattern of the sukuma wiki plant.
slo.skills: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) prepare and observe a hand-lens or microscope specimen of a leaf cross-section; (b) produce a fully annotated scientific diagram of a dicot leaf cross-section; (c) construct evidence-based explanations linking structural features to the sukuma wiki phenomenon.
slo.attitudes: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) appreciate the elegant design of leaf structures as solutions to competing demands (light capture vs. water loss); (b) show curiosity and precision when handling biological specimens and microscopes; (c) value collaborative sense-making when interpreting observations.
slo.keyInquiry: How do plants make their own food?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lesson 1, students anchored their puzzlement in the sukuma wiki phenomenon and generated initial questions on the Driving Question Board. In this lesson, students gather the first direct structural evidence — the internal anatomy of a leaf — that begins to explain why wilting occurs even in moist soil (stomata open under bright light, driving rapid transpiration) and why shade causes pallor (reduced light reaching palisade cells). This evidence advances the class toward a mechanistic explanation of photosynthesis and water movement.
slo.safetyNotes: Razor blades or scalpels used for cross-sections must be handled under direct teacher supervision. Students must not run when moving around lab benches. Microscope lenses should be cleaned only with lens tissue. Wash hands after handling plant material. If iodine solution is used as a staining aid, warn students it is an irritant — avoid contact with eyes and skin.

LESSONS.overview: Students examine freshly cut or prepared cross-sections of a locally available dicot leaf (mango — Mangifera indica — or bean leaf) under a hand lens and, where available, a light microscope. Working in pairs, they produce annotated scientific diagrams identifying the upper and lower epidermis, cuticle, palisade mesophyll, spongy mesophyll, air spaces, stomata, guard cells, and vascular bundles (xylem and phloem). Through structured discussion, they link each layer's position and features to its function: the transparent cuticle and epidermis admit light; the densely packed palisade cells maximise chloroplast exposure; the air spaces in the spongy mesophyll facilitate CO₂ diffusion; the stomata regulate gas exchange and water vapour loss; the xylem supplies water and minerals while phloem exports sugars. Students then revisit the sukuma wiki phenomenon, using their structural evidence to begin explaining why stomata opening at midday drives water loss faster than xylem can supply it — initiating turgor loss and wilting — and why shade reduces the photosynthetic capacity of palisade cells, causing pallor and slow growth. Students update the class Driving Question Board (DQB) with new questions generated by today's evidence.

framework.learnerExperience: Each student receives a whole fresh mango or bean leaf and a hand lens. Without cutting it open, they sketch what they think the inside of the leaf looks like in cross-section — labelling any layers or structures they hypothesise exist and writing one sentence explaining why they think those layers are there. Students then share their prediction sketch with a shoulder partner and note one similarity and one difference between their predictions. The teacher collects 4–5 prediction sketches on the board using magnets (or Blu-Tack) so the class can see the range of ideas. Students are told explicitly: 'We will return to your predictions at the end of today's observation to see which parts were accurate and which we need to revise.'
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute leaves and hand lenses as students settle. Say: 'Before we cut anything open, I want you to think like a detective. Look at the outside of this leaf — feel it, hold it up to the light, look at both surfaces. Now sketch what you think it looks like on the INSIDE — like a slice through a loaf of githeri bread. You have 4 minutes. Go.' [Start timer. Circulate silently for 2 minutes, observing sketches without commenting.] After 2 minutes say: 'Now compare with your partner — find ONE thing you both drew and ONE thing that is different. 30 seconds.' [After 30 seconds] Cold-call 3 pairs: 'Amina and Baraka — what did you both draw?' [10-second wait time after each question before accepting answers.] Select 4–5 sketches that show a range of ideas — from single-layer to multi-layer predictions — and post them on the board. Say: 'Keep these predictions visible. Great scientists revise their ideas when evidence surprises them — let's see what the leaf actually looks like.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Prediction Sketching with Peer Comparison: By committing a prediction to paper before observing, students activate prior knowledge and create a cognitive 'gap' between their mental model and reality. Posting predictions publicly creates accountability and models the scientific norm that initial models are expected to be revised. This directly mirrors how the sukuma wiki student in Nairobi was puzzled — she had a mental model (water in soil = healthy plant) that the evidence contradicted.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher circulates and notes: (1) Do students draw any layers at all, or a single uniform interior? (2) Does any student spontaneously include stomata or veins? (3) Do prediction sketches reference water, light, or gas? This baseline reveals prior knowledge gaps to target during the Observe phase. Observable indicator: every student has a completed prediction sketch with at least one labelled layer before specimens are distributed.

framework.learnerExperience: Students work in pairs at lab benches. Each pair receives: a fresh mango or bean leaf, a pre-prepared microscope slide of a dicot leaf cross-section (or a prepared hand-section made by the teacher using a razor blade and two flat pieces of pith/carrot as guides), a hand lens, and — where microscopes are available — a light microscope set to 40× and 100×. Students follow a structured observation protocol printed on their activity card: Step 1 — Examine the whole leaf with a hand lens: observe the upper vs. lower surface texture, shine, and vein pattern. Record observations using annotated sketches. Step 2 — Examine the cross-section under a hand lens first, then under the microscope (40×, then 100×). Step 3 — On the printed outline diagram provided, shade and label every distinct layer or region they can see, using pencil. Step 4 — At the microscope, locate stomata on the lower epidermis and sketch a single stoma with its two guard cells at high magnification. Students also examine a second slide or use a digital image displayed on the classroom screen showing a stained leaf cross-section so all students — including those without individual microscopes — can observe the full structure. Throughout, the teacher projects the anchor image of the wilting sukuma wiki plant and periodically prompts: 'As you observe each layer, ask yourself — could this layer be connected to why the sukuma wiki wilts?'
framework.teacherMoves: Before observation begins, demonstrate the hand-sectioning technique at the front bench: 'Watch carefully — I am going to show you how to take a very thin slice. The thinner the slice, the more you can see.' [Demonstrate holding the leaf folded between two pieces of carrot, making a firm single stroke with the razor blade.] 'Now — safety first: the blade moves AWAY from your body, always. If you are unsure, raise your hand and I will help you.' Distribute materials. Say: 'You have 20 minutes to complete all four steps on your activity card. I am timing you. Step 1 should take about 4 minutes. Begin.' [Circulate continuously. At the 4-minute mark, prompt:] 'Move to Step 2 now.' [At the 10-minute mark, cold-call one pair:] 'Wanjiru and Omondi — tell the class one layer you can see and where it is.' [10-second wait time.] [At the 15-minute mark, say:] 'Everyone — find the stomata on the lower surface. They may look like tiny mouths or pores. Raise your hand when you have found one.' [Count hands. If fewer than half have found stomata, project the annotated diagram on screen and say:] 'Look at this region here — lower epidermis — scan slowly across this area.' At 20 minutes: 'Pencils down on observation. We will now make sense of what we saw.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Structured Observation Protocol with Scaffolded Diagram Annotation: The printed outline diagram reduces cognitive load so students focus on interpreting what they see rather than on drawing from scratch. The deliberate progression from hand lens → low magnification → high magnification builds the habit of moving from macroscopic to microscopic evidence — a core NGSS Science and Engineering Practice (Planning and Carrying Out Investigations). Projecting the anchor phenomenon image during observation maintains the explanatory goal: students are not just cataloguing structures for their own sake but gathering evidence relevant to a real puzzling event.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks each pair's annotated diagram during circulation. Observable indicators: (1) At least 4 distinct layers are shaded and labelled by the end of Step 2. (2) Every student locates and sketches at least one stoma with guard cells. (3) Students are drawing what they actually see, not what they remember from a textbook (check for personal variation in sketches rather than identical copied diagrams). Red flag: a student who cannot distinguish the upper from lower epidermis — teacher intervenes with: 'Feel both surfaces of the leaf — which side is shinier? Why might that be? Look at which side has more stomata.'

framework.learnerExperience: The class transitions from observation to structured sensemaking. The teacher projects a clean, unlabelled diagram of a dicot leaf cross-section on the board. Using a jigsaw-style expert group activity, each group of 4 is assigned one structural feature (epidermis/cuticle; palisade mesophyll; spongy mesophyll/air spaces; stomata/guard cells; vascular bundles). Each group has 6 minutes to discuss and prepare a 60-second 'function statement' — one sentence describing what their structure looks like, one sentence explaining what it does, and one sentence connecting it to the sukuma wiki phenomenon. Groups present in sequence; the teacher annotates the projected diagram live as each group speaks. After all five presentations, the class co-constructs a summary table (structure → location → adaptation → function → connection to sukuma wiki wilting). The teacher then poses the key synthesis question: 'We now know stomata open to let CO₂ in and O₂ out. But what else moves through open stomata? And if midday sun makes stomata open wide, what does that mean for water loss?' Students write a 3-sentence response in their notebooks individually before the teacher takes a class vote using show-of-hands on the most likely explanation for midday wilting.
framework.teacherMoves: Transition announcement: 'Close your observation notes for now. We are moving to the Explain phase — your job shifts from seeing to explaining.' Assign expert groups by counting off 1–5 around the room. [Post group assignments and their structure on the board.] 'You have 6 minutes in your expert group. I want THREE things from you: what does it look like, what does it do, and how is it connected to the sukuma wiki in our phenomenon? Go.' [Circulate. At 3 minutes, prompt quietly to any group that is off-task:] 'You have 3 minutes left — have you linked your structure to the wilting yet?' [After 6 minutes, call presentations:] 'Epidermis group — 60 seconds. I'll be annotating the diagram as you speak, so be precise with your location words: upper, lower, outer, inner.' After all 5 groups present, pause: 'Before we fill in the summary table, I want everyone to think silently for 15 seconds about this question' [project it on screen]: 'What else moves through an open stoma besides gases?' [15-second wait time — enforce silence explicitly: 'I am waiting for everyone to think, not just the first hands.'] Cold-call 4 students. Then say: 'Write your three-sentence explanation of midday wilting NOW — in your own words, using the structures we just named. Two minutes.' [After 2 minutes, conduct show-of-hands vote and select two contrasting responses to read aloud and compare.]
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Jigsaw Expert Groups with Live Diagram Annotation: The jigsaw structure ensures every student is accountable for deep understanding of at least one structure while receiving peer-taught summaries of the others — building the full picture collaboratively. Live annotation by the teacher models how scientists build diagrams incrementally as evidence accumulates. The 15-second enforced wait time after the synthesis question is critical: it prevents fast responders from dominating and gives all learners — including EAL students — time to formulate thoughts. Connecting every structure back to the sukuma wiki phenomenon prevents the lesson from becoming an isolated anatomy exercise.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) Each expert group produces all three required statements (look, function, phenomenon link) — teacher checks during circulation. (2) In the individual 3-sentence written response, students explicitly name at least one leaf structure (e.g., stomata, guard cells, xylem) AND use the word 'water' in relation to wilting. (3) In the show-of-hands vote, convergence toward the correct explanation (stomata open → water vapour exits → turgor lost → wilting) is the target. If fewer than 60% of the class converges, the teacher re-teaches using a targeted analogy: 'Think of the stomata as a window in a nyumba — open it on a hot Nairobi afternoon and what happens to the air inside?'

framework.learnerExperience: Students return to the class Driving Question Board (established in Lesson 1). Each student receives two sticky notes. On the first (yellow), they write one NEW question that today's leaf structure observation has generated — something they now wonder about that they did not wonder about before (e.g., 'What controls whether stomata open or close?', 'Do stomata open at night?', 'Why are palisade cells packed so tightly at the top?'). On the second (green), they write one question from Lesson 1's DQB that they now feel they can partially answer based on today's evidence, and they write a brief partial answer. Students post their sticky notes on the DQB in the relevant clusters (established in Lesson 1). The class spends 3 minutes doing a silent gallery walk of the new additions. The teacher then reads out 2–3 of the new yellow-note questions that anticipate upcoming lesson content (stomatal opening mechanism, chlorophyll and light, photosynthesis equation) and says: 'These questions are excellent. They are exactly what we will be investigating over the next several lessons. Keep them in mind.'
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute two sticky notes per student. Say: 'The DQB is our class brain — it shows what we know and what we still need to find out. You have 3 minutes: yellow note = a brand new question today's evidence gave you; green note = a question from Lesson 1 you can now partially answer. Write NOW — no talking.' [After 3 minutes:] 'Post your notes in the matching cluster on the board. If you are not sure which cluster, make a new one.' [Allow 2 minutes for posting.] 'Now — 3 minutes of silent gallery walk. Read what your classmates wrote. Put a small dot on any yellow-note question that you also wonder about.' [After gallery walk:] 'Let me read three questions that I think will drive our next three lessons.' [Read selected questions slowly and clearly.] 'These are the questions that real plant biologists in Kenya — like researchers at KARI in Nairobi — are also asking. Hold onto them.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Two-Colour DQB Protocol (New Questions / Partial Answers): Distinguishing between new questions (yellow) and partial answers (green) makes the growth of understanding visible — students literally see the board filling with green as the unit progresses. The dot-voting during the gallery walk democratises curiosity: students see that their peers share the same wonders, building a community of inquiry. Referencing Kenyan researchers (KARI — Kenya Agricultural and Livestock Research Organisation) grounds the questions in a real, local scientific community.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) Every student posts at least one yellow and one green sticky note. (2) Yellow notes reference specific structures observed today (stomata, guard cells, etc.) rather than vague general questions. (3) Green notes contain a partial mechanistic answer — not just 'because of water' but 'because stomata are open and water vapour escapes.' Teacher collects and photographs the DQB at the end of each lesson to track conceptual progression across the unit.

framework.learnerExperience: Students retrieve their initial phenomenon model from Lesson 1 (a sketch explaining why the sukuma wiki wilts). They now REVISE this model to incorporate today's structural evidence. Using coloured pencils, they add a cross-section of a leaf showing at least: the epidermis/cuticle, palisade mesophyll, spongy mesophyll, stomata with guard cells, and a vascular bundle. They draw arrows showing: (a) light entering from above through the epidermis; (b) water moving up through the xylem; (c) water vapour exiting through the stoma. They annotate each arrow with a brief label. Below their model, students write a 'Model Revision Statement': 'In Lesson 1 I thought ____ . Now I have added ____ because today's evidence showed ____ . My model still cannot explain ____ .' The unresolved part — typically the mechanism of stomatal opening and why it is triggered by light — is circled in red as a 'placeholder for future lessons.' Models are kept in the student's personal Biology portfolio for cumulative revision throughout the unit.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Get out your Lesson 1 model. You are about to upgrade it — like updating an app on your phone when new information becomes available. This is exactly what scientists do.' [Allow 30 seconds for students to find their Lesson 1 models.] 'You have 8 minutes. Add the leaf cross-section to your model. Add arrows for light, water, and water vapour. Write your Model Revision Statement at the bottom. If you finish early, add a second arrow showing sugars moving DOWN through the phloem — we'll explain that fully in a future lesson.' [Circulate. At 5 minutes, prompt:] 'Make sure your Model Revision Statement has all three parts — what you thought before, what you added, what you still can't explain.' [At 8 minutes:] 'Hold up your revised model — I want to see that every model now has a cross-section with arrows.' [Do a quick visual scan.] 'Excellent. Put a red circle around the part of your model you cannot yet explain. That red circle is your personal research question for this unit. We will fill it in together.' [Collect one strong model and one weak model — anonymously — to display next lesson as 'Model A' and 'Model B' for the class to compare and improve.]
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision with Explicit Revision Statements: Requiring a written 'Model Revision Statement' makes the cognitive process of updating a mental model explicit and metacognitive — students articulate what changed and why, rather than simply redrawing. The 'red circle placeholder' technique normalises scientific incompleteness: it is not a failure to not yet understand stomatal mechanics; it is an honest scientific statement of the boundaries of current evidence. This directly mirrors how the sukuma wiki student's understanding is deepening — she can now explain WHAT is happening structurally, but not yet fully WHY stomata open in response to light.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) Revised model includes a recognisable leaf cross-section with at least 3 named layers. (2) At least two directional arrows are present with labels (water up, water vapour out, or light in). (3) Model Revision Statement contains all three required components — prior belief, new addition, unresolved question. (4) The 'red circle' is present and the unresolved question is mechanistic (e.g., 'I still can't explain what makes the guard cells open the stoma') rather than trivial. Teacher uses the two anonymously collected models at the start of Lesson 3 as a 'Model Clinic' to sharpen the class's modelling skills.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, reflect on the following questions: (1) During the Observe phase, how many pairs successfully located stomata on the lower epidermis under the hand lens or microscope? If fewer than half succeeded, consider preparing pre-mounted slides or a printed photomicrograph as a fallback for Lesson 3. (2) In the Explain phase, did expert groups make the three-part connection (structure → function → phenomenon) or did they stop at function? If connections to the wilting phenomenon were weak, open Lesson 3 with a targeted question: 'Yesterday you described what the stomata do — today I want us to explain WHY that matters to our sukuma wiki.' (3) Were yellow DQB sticky notes genuinely mechanistic (e.g., 'What makes guard cells swell?') or superficial (e.g., 'Why are plants green?')? Mechanistic questions signal readiness for Lesson 3's deeper dive into chloroplasts and pigments. (4) Review 5–6 revised models at random: are arrows directional and labelled? Are Model Revision Statements specific? Students who write 'I still don't understand photosynthesis' need more scaffolding in their revision statements — prompt them to name a specific structure they observed that they cannot yet fully explain. (5) Was the 15-second wait time enforced consistently? Note which students have not yet voluntarily contributed to whole-class discussion and cold-call them specifically in Lesson 3.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Students examined the internal cross-section of a dicot leaf (mango or bean) using a hand lens and microscope, identifying the upper epidermis, cuticle, palisade mesophyll, spongy mesophyll, air spaces, lower epidermis, stomata with guard cells, and vascular bundles (xylem and phloem).
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Each leaf layer is structurally adapted to a specific function: the transparent cuticle/epidermis admits light while reducing water loss; the densely packed palisade mesophyll maximises chloroplast exposure to light; the spongy mesophyll provides air spaces for CO₂ and O₂ diffusion; stomata regulate gas exchange and water vapour loss; xylem transports water and minerals upward while phloem transports sugars downward.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: The sukuma wiki wilts at midday even in moist soil because strong light triggers stomata to open wide for gas exchange (CO₂ in, O₂ out), simultaneously allowing rapid water vapour loss (transpiration) through the lower epidermis. When the rate of water vapour loss through open stomata exceeds the rate at which xylem can deliver water from the roots, leaf cells lose turgor pressure and the plant wilts. At night, stomata close, transpiration stops, xylem catches up, turgor is restored, and the plant recovers — explaining the daily wilting-and-recovery cycle.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: leaf structure, dicot leaf cross-section, epidermis, cuticle, palisade mesophyll, spongy mesophyll, stomata, guard cells, vascular bundle, xylem, phloem, transpiration, turgor pressure, photosynthesis, wilting, sukuma wiki, plant nutrition, gas exchange, chloroplast, adaptation, microscopy, Biology Grade 10, Kenya CBC, Sub-Strand 2.1

LESSONS.title: Raw Materials for Photosynthesis: What Does a Plant Actually Need?
LESSONS.aresKeywords: photosynthesis, raw materials, carbon dioxide, water, light energy, chlorophyll, word equation, chemical equation, limiting factor, autotrophic nutrition

slo.purpose: Learners identify the four raw materials required for photosynthesis, write the word and chemical equations, and use those raw materials to begin explaining why the sukuma wiki wilts at midday and turns pale in shade.
slo.knowledge: Learners will be able to: (1) state the four raw materials for photosynthesis — carbon dioxide, water, light energy, and chlorophyll; (2) write the word equation for photosynthesis; (3) write the balanced chemical equation for photosynthesis; (4) identify which raw materials are implicated in midday wilting and which are implicated in pallor under shade.
slo.skills: Learners will practise structured reading for information extraction, guided scientific discussion, equation writing, and evidence-based reasoning to link raw materials to observable symptoms in the anchoring phenomenon.
slo.attitudes: Learners develop curiosity about how everyday observations — such as a wilting kitchen-garden plant — can be explained through chemistry and biology, and appreciate the relevance of plant science to Kenyan food security and agriculture.
slo.keyInquiry: How do plants make their own food? (KICD Key Inquiry Question 1)
slo.purposeInStoryline: Lesson 2 revealed the internal architecture that enables photosynthesis. Lesson 3 now identifies WHAT the plant must gather to run photosynthesis, providing the conceptual bridge between structure and process. This makes the wilting and pallor symptoms causally explicable — wilting relates to water and high light-driven transpiration, pallor relates to reduced light and therefore reduced chlorophyll synthesis — setting up the starch-test evidence in Lesson 4.
slo.safety3otes: No hazardous chemicals are used in this lesson. If printed text resources are distributed, ensure they are legible. Learners with visual impairments should be seated near the board. No special safety precautions are required beyond standard classroom conduct.

LESSONS.overview: The lesson opens by revisiting the sukuma wiki phenomenon and asking learners to predict what inputs the plant needs before any structured reading occurs. Learners then work in pairs through a two-page structured reading resource (provided as a printed handout or written on the board in resource-limited settings) that presents evidence for each raw material — CO2 entry via stomata, water uptake via roots and xylem, light absorption by chlorophyll, and the role of chlorophyll as a pigment. Guided whole-class discussion consolidates understanding. Learners write the word equation and chemical equation in their notebooks, then apply their new knowledge back to the phenomenon by categorising which raw materials explain wilting and which explain pallor on the Driving Question Board.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners open their notebooks to their Lesson 1 predictions. The teacher displays or reads aloud two sentences from the original phenomenon description: (1) The sukuma wiki leaves wilt at midday even though the soil is moist. (2) In shade the wilting stops but the leaves turn yellowish-green and growth slows. Each learner writes a response to the prompt: List at least two things you think the plant needs in order to stay green and keep growing. Give a reason for each thing you list. Learners share with a partner before two or three pairs volunteer answers to the class. Responses are recorded in two columns on the board under the headings NEEDED TO STAY GREEN and NEEDED TO GROW. No answers are confirmed or rejected yet — they are treated as hypotheses to be tested against the reading.
framework.teacherMoves: Display or write the two phenomenon sentences on the board. Distribute notebooks prompt or write it on the board: List at least two things you think the plant needs to stay green and keep growing — give a reason for each. Say: Think back to the leaf structure we studied last lesson. You saw where water comes in, where light enters, and where gases move. Use that knowledge to predict what raw materials photosynthesis might need. Allow 2 minutes of individual silent writing. Then say: Compare your list with your partner — do you agree? You have 1 minute. [WAIT TIME: 1 minute pair discussion.] Cold-call two pairs. Record their suggestions in the two columns without evaluating. Say: We are going to read some evidence now that will help us decide which of these predictions are correct and which need to change.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Predict-then-read structure activates prior knowledge from Lesson 2 (leaf anatomy) and creates cognitive hooks for the reading. Recording predictions publicly makes thinking visible and creates accountability for revision during sense-making.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans the board columns for misconceptions — for example, learners who list soil nutrients as the only input, or who omit light. These gaps are noted mentally to probe during the Explain phase. Teacher circulates during pair discussion and listens for reasoning quality.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners receive the structured reading handout titled Evidence for the Raw Materials of Photosynthesis (or follow along as the teacher reads from the board in low-resource settings). The handout contains four short evidence paragraphs, one for each raw material, each linked to a real Kenyan context: (A) Carbon dioxide — scientists at the University of Nairobi have measured CO2 concentrations inside and outside leaf chambers; CO2 enters through stomata as shown in Lesson 2. (B) Water — water travels from Nairobi City Water and Sewerage Company boreholes through soil into plant roots, up the xylem, into mesophyll cells; without water the stroma cannot carry out reactions. (C) Light energy — solar radiation data from the Kenya Meteorological Department shows peak irradiance in Nairobi between 11:00 and 14:00, coinciding exactly with the wilting window observed by the student. (D) Chlorophyll — the green pigment in chloroplasts absorbs red and blue light; plants in shade receive less light so less chlorophyll is stimulated and, over time, less chlorophyll is synthesised, causing pallor. Learners read silently and annotate using the code: circle each raw material name, underline the sentence that tells you where or how the plant gets it, and place a question mark next to anything they do not understand.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute handouts or direct attention to the board. Say: Read each paragraph carefully. As you read, circle every raw material you find, underline where or how the plant gets it, and put a question mark next to anything confusing. You have 6 minutes to read and annotate silently. [WAIT TIME: 6 minutes silent reading.] After 6 minutes say: Turn to your partner — share one thing you circled and one thing you put a question mark next to. [WAIT TIME: 2 minutes pair share.] Then ask the class: What question marks came up? Collect two or three genuine confusion points and write them on the board labelled QUESTIONS FROM THE READING. Say: We will address every one of these during our discussion right now.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Structured annotation (circle, underline, question mark) is a disciplinary literacy strategy that trains learners to read scientific text actively rather than passively. Collecting question marks publicly validates confusion as a scientific starting point and models how scientists track unknowns.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher circulates during silent reading and checks annotations. Are learners circling all four raw materials or only the obvious ones? Are question marks clustered around the chemical equation or around a specific material? This informs the depth of explanation needed in the Explain phase.

framework.learnerExperience: The teacher leads a 12-minute guided discussion that moves through three steps: (1) Confirming the four raw materials — CO2, H2O, light energy, chlorophyll — with learners adding or correcting their prediction lists. (2) Building the word equation together: learners suggest inputs and outputs; the teacher scribes on the board: carbon dioxide plus water plus light energy in the presence of chlorophyll yields glucose plus oxygen. Learners copy this into their notebooks. (3) Building the chemical equation: the teacher writes the unbalanced equation on the board and invites learners to count atoms together — 6CO2 plus 6H2O yields C6H12O6 plus 6O2 with light energy and chlorophyll shown above the arrow. Learners copy the balanced equation and label each term. The teacher then poses the application question: Looking at the four raw materials, which one or ones do you think are most directly responsible for the wilting at midday? Which are responsible for the pallor in shade? Learners discuss in pairs for 90 seconds before sharing answers. The teacher guides the class to the following reasoning: wilting is linked to water (high transpiration rate at peak solar irradiance exceeds water supply to leaves — even though soil is moist, the rate of loss outpaces uptake) and to light intensity driving transpiration; pallor and slow growth are linked to reduced light energy reaching chlorophyll and therefore reduced glucose synthesis.
framework.teacherMoves: Return to the prediction columns on the board. Say: Let us check your predictions. Which of these items appear in what you read? Cross out anything the evidence does not support and add anything you missed. Give learners 1 minute to update their notebooks. Then say: I am going to write a sentence on the board and I need you to help me complete it. The raw materials for photosynthesis are — pause — who can give me the first one? Build the word equation collaboratively, scribing exactly what learners say and correcting gently. Say: Now I want to write this as a chemical equation. Has anyone seen chemical formulas before? Do not worry if not — we will build it together by counting atoms. Work through the balancing step by step, narrating your thinking aloud: I need 6 carbons on the left so I use 6CO2. After the equation is complete, say: Now the big question — and I want you to think carefully before you answer. [WAIT TIME: pause 5 seconds of silence.] Of these four raw materials, which one is most directly connected to the wilting? Which to the pallor? Discuss with your partner. [WAIT TIME: 90 seconds.] Cold-call two pairs. Probe further: How does more sunlight lead to more water loss — think about the stomata you saw in Lesson 2. Guide learners to connect stomatal opening at high light intensity to increased transpiration. Say: This is powerful — the same light that drives photosynthesis also drives the transpiration that causes wilting. That is a real scientific tension.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Collaborative equation building (rather than teacher telling) positions learners as co-constructors of knowledge. The application question creates a bridge between abstract chemistry and the lived phenomenon, using the leaf structure knowledge from Lesson 2 as a reasoning scaffold. The explicit connection — light drives both photosynthesis and transpiration — is a high-leverage insight that advances the storyline.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher listens to pair discussions and cold-call responses to assess whether learners can reason causally (not just recall). Probe questions: Why does the plant not simply close its stomata during the day? What would happen to CO2 supply if stomata closed? These questions reveal depth of understanding and surface the stomatal dilemma that will resurface in Lesson 5.

framework.learnerExperience: Each learner receives two sticky notes (or tears a small piece of paper into two). On the first note they write one question that has been answered today about why sukuma wiki wilts or turns pale. On the second note they write one new question the lesson has raised for them. Learners post their notes in the appropriate sections of the class DQB — an ANSWERED section and a NEW QUESTIONS section. The teacher reads three to five new questions aloud and tags them with lesson numbers from the storyline to show the class when those questions will be addressed. The class identifies the two most important unanswered questions by show of hands and circles them on the DQB.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute two sticky notes per learner. Say: On the first note write one question that you feel you can now answer because of what we learned today — start it with the words: I can now explain why... On the second note write one brand-new question this lesson has raised for you — something you are now curious about that you were not thinking about before class. Give 2 minutes for writing. [WAIT TIME: 2 minutes.] Direct learners to post their notes. Read three new questions aloud. Say: These are excellent scientific questions. Let me show you where in our unit each of these will be answered. Point to the storyline summary posted on the wall. Tag the oxygen question to Lesson 5, the glucose question to Lesson 6, the mineral question to Lesson 9. Say: Every question you have is part of the science of this unit. We will get to all of them.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The DQB move makes the storyline progression visible and emotionally rewarding — learners see their confusion transforming into answered questions across lessons. Labelling new questions with future lesson numbers builds anticipation and coherent narrative across the unit.
framework.formativeAssessment: The ANSWERED sticky notes are the primary formative evidence. Teacher reads them after class to check whether learners are correctly attributing wilting to water loss and light-driven transpiration and pallor to reduced light and chlorophyll activity. Misconceptions on these notes inform the opening review of Lesson 4.

framework.learnerExperience: In the final 5 minutes learners open to a fresh notebook page and draw a quick rough diagram — not a neat illustration but a thinking tool — showing the sukuma wiki plant with four arrows pointing inward, each labelled with one raw material and where it enters or comes from: CO2 from the air through stomata, H2O from the soil through roots and xylem, light energy from the sun onto the leaf surface, and chlorophyll inside the chloroplasts of the palisade mesophyll. They add two output arrows labelled glucose and oxygen. Below the diagram they write one sentence: This diagram shows that without enough water or light reaching the leaf the plant cannot complete photosynthesis. Learners are told this rough model will be expanded in every subsequent lesson until Lesson 10 when it becomes their final annotated explanation of the whole phenomenon.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: I want you to build the first version of a model you will keep adding to all the way to Lesson 10. Open a fresh page — label it MY PHOTOSYNTHESIS MODEL — VERSION 1. Draw a simple outline of the sukuma wiki plant. Add four arrows for the raw materials coming IN and two arrows for the products going OUT. Label everything. You have 4 minutes. [WAIT TIME: 4 minutes.] As learners draw, circulate and check that all four inputs are present and correctly sourced. After 4 minutes say: Write one sentence below your diagram beginning with: This diagram shows that... Cold-call one learner to read their sentence. Affirm and correct gently. Say: Next lesson we will use iodine solution and ethanol to test a real leaf and find out whether photosynthesis actually produced starch — and your model will get its first piece of experimental evidence.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The living model strategy (a diagram that is deliberately incomplete and revised across lessons) embeds the metacognitive habit of treating scientific knowledge as provisional and cumulative. It also creates a personal artefact that spans the entire unit, making the storyline tangible for each learner.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher does a quick 30-second gallery walk during the drawing phase, checking that learners have placed chlorophyll inside the chloroplast (not outside the plant) and that xylem is mentioned as the water pathway — two concepts from Lesson 2 that should now be integrated. Any learner who omits chlorophyll or places it incorrectly receives a quiet one-to-one prompt before the lesson ends.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson ask yourself: (1) Could learners reason causally about which raw material causes wilting versus pallor, or did they simply recall the four items as a list? If causal reasoning was weak, begin Lesson 4 with a quick Think-Pair-Share on the question: Why does MORE sunlight cause LESS water in the leaf? (2) Were the chemical equation steps scaffolded enough, or did equation-writing create anxiety that distracted from the conceptual goals? If so, consider providing a partially completed equation frame in Lesson 4 activities. (3) Did the DQB new-question notes reveal any persistent misconceptions — for example, that chlorophyll is a raw material consumed by the reaction rather than a catalyst-like pigment? If so, address this at the opening of Lesson 5 when chlorophyll is discussed in the context of light absorption. (4) Were Kenyan contexts (Kenya Meteorological Department irradiance data, University of Nairobi research) engaging and credible to learners, or did they feel forced? Adjust the framing in future lessons based on learner response.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: We read evidence showing that carbon dioxide enters the leaf through stomata, water travels from the soil up the xylem to mesophyll cells, light energy from the sun peaks in Nairobi between 11:00 and 14:00 coinciding with wilting, and chlorophyll in the palisade mesophyll absorbs that light.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Photosynthesis requires four raw materials: carbon dioxide, water, light energy, and chlorophyll. The word equation is: carbon dioxide plus water in the presence of light energy and chlorophyll yields glucose plus oxygen. The balanced chemical equation is: 6CO2 plus 6H2O yields C6H12O6 plus 6O2. Chlorophyll is not consumed in the reaction but is essential as a light-absorbing pigment.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: The sukuma wiki wilts at midday because peak solar irradiance causes stomata to open wide for CO2 uptake, which simultaneously increases transpiration so water leaves the leaf faster than the xylem can replace it — even when soil is moist. The pallor and slow growth in shade are explained by reduced light energy reaching chlorophyll, which reduces the rate of photosynthesis and therefore reduces glucose production, slowing growth and reducing the stimulus for chlorophyll synthesis.

LESSONS.title: Starch Test Practical: Finding Evidence That Chlorophyll and Light Are Both Needed for Photosynthesis
LESSONS.aresKeywords: photosynthesis, starch test, iodine solution, ethanol decolourisation, variegated leaf, chlorophyll, light requirement, evidence, limiting factor

slo.purpose: Learners carry out the standard starch test on a variegated or partially shaded leaf to generate direct observational evidence that both chlorophyll and light are required for photosynthesis to produce starch.
slo.knowledge: Learners will be able to: (1) state that starch is a product of photosynthesis stored in leaves; (2) describe the steps of the starch test using hot ethanol and iodine solution; (3) explain that blue-black colouration in the iodine test indicates the presence of starch; (4) conclude that only regions of a leaf that are green AND were illuminated produce starch, providing evidence that both chlorophyll and light are necessary for photosynthesis.
slo.skills: Safe laboratory technique including handling hot water baths and ethanol; careful observation and recording of colour changes; scientific inference from experimental results; annotating diagrams of leaf results.
slo.attitudes: Curiosity and open-mindedness when results challenge prior assumptions; honesty in recording actual observations rather than expected ones; appreciation of practical evidence as the foundation of scientific knowledge.
slo.keyInquiry: How do plants make their own food? (Key Inquiry Question 1) — this lesson provides physical evidence from inside the leaf itself that answers part of this question.
slo.purposeInStoryline: Lessons 1 to 3 built the conceptual framework: the sukuma wiki wilts because of water and light, and requires CO2, H2O, light, and chlorophyll to photosynthesise. This lesson shifts from reading and discussion to hands-on evidence generation. Learners see with their own eyes that removing chlorophyll (white sections of a variegated leaf) or removing light (covered section) both prevent starch production. This is the first concrete, self-generated evidence in the sequence and directly justifies why the pale, shaded sukuma wiki produces less food. It also sets up Lessons 5 and 6 which explain the biochemical mechanism behind these observations.
slo.safety4otes: SAFETY: Ethanol is highly flammable — NO naked flames when ethanol is in use. Use an electric hot plate or a pre-boiled hot water bath maintained away from the burner. Learners must wear eye protection when handling iodine solution and hot water. Hot ethanol tubes must be handled with tongs or a cloth. Iodine solution stains skin and clothing — warn learners. If a variegated plant (e.g., Coleus or variegated Sansevieria) is not available, a bean or kale leaf partially covered with black paper or aluminium foil for 48 hours before the lesson is an equally valid and locally accessible alternative.

LESSONS.overview: Learners begin by predicting which parts of a variegated or partially shaded leaf they expect to contain starch and why, linking back to the raw materials identified in Lesson 3. They then carry out the classic starch test — killing the leaf in boiling water, decolourising in hot ethanol, washing, and applying iodine solution — and record colour changes on a diagram.
