In the Explain phase they match their results to the prediction, construct a conclusion linking chlorophyll and light to starch production, and update the Driving Question Board with new evidence. A brief model-building task asks learners to annotate a simple leaf cross-section diagram to show where photosynthesis is and is not occurring based on their results.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are shown either (a) a real variegated leaf (e.g., from a Coleus plant available in many Nairobi school gardens or a variegated Sansevieria from a household) placed on a white sheet of paper, OR (b) a diagram of a leaf showing a white/yellow region, a green region, and a green region that was covered with black paper for 48 hours. Working individually for 2 minutes and then sharing with a partner, each learner draws and colours the leaf in their notebook, labelling the three zones, and writes a prediction answering: Zone 1 — white/no-chlorophyll region: will starch be present? Zone 2 — green uncovered region: will starch be present? Zone 3 — green covered region: will starch be present? Learners must justify each prediction using the word equation from Lesson 3, explicitly naming which raw material is absent in each zone.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the leaf or diagram on the board. Say: 'Before we do any chemistry, I want you to think like a scientist. Look at this leaf carefully. It has three different zones. Using only what we learned last lesson about the raw materials for photosynthesis, predict in your notebook which parts you expect to contain starch after our test — and give a reason for each one.' Allow 2 minutes of silent individual writing. WAIT TIME: Do not speak or prompt during these 2 minutes. Then say: 'Share your prediction with the person next to you. Do you agree? If not, whose reasoning is stronger?' After 1 minute of pair talk, cold-call two or three pairs to share contrasting predictions. Record the class predictions in two columns on the board — STARCH PRESENT and NO STARCH — against each zone. Say: 'We are going to keep these predictions visible on the board. At the end of the practical, we will come back and see who the evidence supports.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Prediction-justification routine forces learners to apply prior knowledge (raw materials from Lesson 3) to a new situation. Recording predictions publicly on the board creates accountability and cognitive dissonance when results differ from expectations, which drives deeper explanation in the Explain phase.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher circulates during individual writing and reads predictions over shoulders. Listen for: (1) correct reasoning — white zone lacks chlorophyll so no starch; covered zone lacks light so no starch; green uncovered zone has both so starch present; (2) misconceptions — some learners may predict that the covered green zone still makes starch because it is still green (confusing chlorophyll presence with chlorophyll function). Note these learners for targeted questioning during the Explain phase.

framework.learnerExperience: Groups of 3 to 4 carry out the starch test following a step-by-step method card provided by the teacher. Steps: (1) Place the leaf in boiling water for 2 minutes to kill cells and stop enzyme activity — observe that the leaf becomes limp and darker. (2) Transfer the leaf using tongs into a tube of ethanol placed in a hot water bath (ethanol must NOT be heated directly over a flame) for 5 to 7 minutes until the leaf is decolourised and the ethanol turns green — observe the colour of both the leaf and the ethanol. (3) Remove the leaf, rinse gently in warm water to soften it, and spread flat on a white tile or petri dish. (4) Flood the leaf with iodine solution for 1 to 2 minutes. (5) Observe and record the colour of each zone immediately. Learners record results by: (a) writing the colour of each zone in a results table (Zone, Colour after iodine, Starch present YES or NO); (b) drawing and colouring an accurate diagram of the leaf after iodine treatment in their notebooks.
framework.teacherMoves: Before the practical begins, demonstrate Step 1 and Step 2 with one leaf while the class watches, narrating the safety points: 'Notice I am keeping the ethanol tube in the hot water bath — never over a flame. I am using tongs, not my fingers, to handle the hot tube. Everyone, check that your burner or hot plate is switched off before you pick up any ethanol.' Circulate constantly during the practical. At the decolourisation step, prompt: 'What colour is the ethanol turning? Why do you think that is happening?' WAIT TIME: pause 10 seconds after this question before accepting answers. At the iodine step, instruct learners to apply iodine and watch in real time: 'Do not look away — the colour change happens quickly. Record exactly what you see, even if it surprises you.' After results are recorded, ask: 'Compare your leaf diagram to the predictions still on the board. What do you notice?' Allow 1 minute of silent comparison before moving to the Explain phase.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Real-time observation of colour change in a self-conducted experiment creates high cognitive engagement. The instruction to record even surprising results builds scientific honesty. Comparing the physical leaf to the board predictions creates the conceptual tension that drives the Explain phase.
framework.formativeAssessment: Check results tables as groups complete them. Correct errors immediately — for example, if a group records the covered green zone as blue-black, ask them to check whether that section was truly covered (it may not have been, which is itself a useful discussion point about experimental control). Listen for spontaneous explanations among learners — these reveal depth of understanding. Ask any group that finishes early: 'If we had used a completely yellow leaf with no chlorophyll anywhere, what result would you predict? What about a completely green leaf that had been in the dark for 48 hours?'

framework.learnerExperience: Whole-class discussion and individual written conclusion. The teacher guides learners to construct a three-part evidence-based conclusion by working through the results zone by zone. Learners then write the conclusion in their notebooks in the following structured format: OBSERVATION (what colour appeared), INFERENCE (what this tells us about starch), CONCLUSION (what this tells us about the requirement for photosynthesis). After the structured conclusion, learners revisit the sukuma wiki phenomenon: they write one sentence connecting their practical result to why the shaded sukuma wiki turned pale and grew slowly — linking lack of light to reduced starch and glucose production, which slows growth. Finally, a guided question asks: 'Our result shows that BOTH chlorophyll and light are needed. From Lesson 3, we know CO2 and water are also needed. How would you design an experiment to test whether CO2 is essential?' Learners briefly discuss in pairs.
framework.teacherMoves: Project or draw a three-column table on the board: Zone, Colour after iodine, Conclusion. Work through the table as a class. For the white zone, say: 'Everyone who got no colour change here — what does that tell us? What was missing in this zone?' WAIT TIME: 10 seconds. Accept answers. Write the class consensus. Then say: 'So removing the green pigment alone was enough to stop starch production. What does that prove about chlorophyll?' For the covered green zone, say: 'This zone had chlorophyll — it was still green — but it was kept in the dark. What happened?' WAIT TIME: 10 seconds. Guide learners to state: light is required even when chlorophyll is present. Say: 'This is important. Chlorophyll alone is not enough. Light alone is not enough. Both must be present at the same time.' Write this on the board as a key statement. Then connect to the phenomenon: 'Remember our sukuma wiki under the mango tree — it had chlorophyll, but what was reduced?' Elicit: light intensity. Say: 'And our results just proved that reduced light means reduced starch production. That is why the shaded plant grew slowly.' Close by acknowledging the DQB: 'We now have laboratory evidence, not just a hypothesis, for part of our driving question. Let us update the board.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Structured conclusion writing (Observation - Inference - Conclusion) scaffolds scientific reasoning and prevents learners from writing vague statements. Connecting the result to the phenomenon at the end of the Explain phase maintains the storyline thread. The extension question about CO2 foreshadows Lesson 7 (the investigation) and keeps inquiry open.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect written conclusions at the end of the lesson or photograph them for review. Look for: (1) correct use of evidence — does the learner cite the actual colour change observed, not just state what they expected? (2) correct causal language — does the learner say chlorophyll is REQUIRED rather than just present? (3) connection to the phenomenon — can the learner transfer the lab result to the sukuma wiki context? Common error to watch for: learners writing that the white zone turned blue-black because it had no starch — they must explain WHY it had no starch (no chlorophyll, so no photosynthesis).

framework.learnerExperience: Each group receives two sticky notes (different colours: one for EVIDENCE WE NOW HAVE, one for NEW QUESTIONS RAISED). Groups write: on the first note, one piece of evidence from todays practical that helps answer the driving question; on the second note, one new question the practical has raised (for example: If chlorophyll absorbs light, which colours of light work best? What exactly does chlorophyll do with light energy?). Groups post their notes on the class DQB under the appropriate columns. The teacher reads selected notes aloud and the class votes on which new question they most want to answer, flagging it for Lesson 5.
framework.teacherMoves: Point to the DQB and say: 'In Lesson 1 we put up questions about why sukuma wiki wilts and why shade causes pallor. In Lesson 3 we started answering some of those. Today we have real experimental evidence. Take 2 minutes — one evidence note, one new question note.' WAIT TIME: allow genuine silent writing for 2 minutes. As groups post notes, read two or three aloud. Say: 'Look at the new questions people are asking — they want to know what chlorophyll actually DOES with light. That is exactly what we will investigate in Lesson 5. Science works this way: answering one question always opens new ones.' Leave all notes visible for the remainder of the unit.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The DQB routine makes the epistemic progress of the class visible — learners see questions being answered and new ones generated, modelling authentic scientific practice. The voting on the next question gives learners agency in the inquiry sequence.
framework.formativeAssessment: Read all posted evidence notes. Check that evidence notes cite the starch test result specifically, not just general statements. If any note says only 'plants need light' without referencing the experimental observation, ask that group to revise: 'What did you actually see in the iodine test that tells you that?' This ensures learners are reasoning from evidence rather than recalling prior knowledge.

framework.learnerExperience: Individual task (5 minutes). Learners receive or draw a simple outline of a leaf cross-section with three zones marked (matching the three zones of the variegated or covered leaf). Using coloured pencils, learners annotate: (1) which zones contain chlorophyll; (2) which zones received light; (3) which zones produced starch (shade with blue-black cross-hatching); (4) a brief written label for each zone explaining why starch is or is not present, using the word equation from Lesson 3 as a reference. This annotated diagram becomes part of their running model that will be extended in Lessons 5, 6, and 10.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'I want you to build a small model in your notebook that captures everything we just proved. Draw or trace the leaf outline I have given you. Use colour to show your results, and then explain each zone in writing using the word equation. This diagram will be the starting point for the bigger model we build at the end of the unit.' Circulate and prompt: 'Show me which zone proves that chlorophyll alone is not enough. Now show me which zone proves that light alone is not enough.' WAIT TIME: after each prompt, pause 8 seconds before offering further guidance. Close the lesson by returning to the phenomenon: 'Next lesson we will ask: what is chlorophyll actually doing with that light — and that will finally start to explain the oxygen bubbles and the energy the sukuma wiki needs to grow.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The annotated cross-section diagram serves as a cumulative model artefact — learners will add the light-dependent and light-independent reactions to it in Lessons 5 and 6, and it will form the basis of the final synthesis model in Lesson 10. Framing it as a growing model sustains motivation across the unit.
framework.formativeAssessment: Check that annotations correctly assign the absence of starch to the correct missing variable in each zone. A learner who labels the covered green zone as lacking starch because of no chlorophyll has a misconception that must be corrected before Lesson 5. Exit check: ask two or three learners to read their label for Zone 3 (covered green) aloud — the label must reference light as the missing factor, not chlorophyll.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, consider: (1) Did all groups achieve clear decolourisation? If not, was the ethanol bath hot enough and was sufficient time allowed? Incomplete decolourisation makes iodine results ambiguous — plan to pre-prepare decolourised leaves as backup. (2) Did learners distinguish between chlorophyll being PRESENT and chlorophyll being FUNCTIONAL — this is a subtle but important conceptual point that underpins Lesson 5. (3) Were the written conclusions evidence-based or did learners just repeat what they predicted? If learners struggled to write conclusions from evidence, build in a sentence-starter scaffold next time: 'The result showed that the [zone] turned [colour], which means starch was [present or absent], which proves that [raw material] is required for photosynthesis because...' (4) Which DQB questions about what chlorophyll does with light were most frequently asked? Use these to personalise the opening of Lesson 5.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: When iodine solution was applied to a decolourised variegated or partially covered leaf: the white region (no chlorophyll) remained brown-orange; the covered green region (chlorophyll present but no light) remained brown-orange; the green uncovered region (chlorophyll present and light available) turned blue-black, indicating starch was present only in that zone.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Both chlorophyll AND light must be present simultaneously for photosynthesis to occur and for starch to be produced in a leaf. Removing either one — even if the other is present — is sufficient to prevent photosynthesis. This is direct experimental evidence, not just a stated fact.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: The shaded sukuma wiki in the phenomenon turns pale and grows slowly because, even though it retains chlorophyll, reduced light intensity under the mango tree limits photosynthesis and therefore reduces the glucose and starch available for growth — consistent with the starch test result showing that the covered green zone (chlorophyll present, light absent) produces no starch.

LESSONS.title: Light-Dependent Reactions: Where the Sun's Energy Enters the Plant

slo.purpose: To trace the light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis — including light absorption by chlorophyll, photolysis of water, production of ATP and NADPH, and release of oxygen — and connect these molecular events to the wilting, recovery, and pallor observed in the sukuma wiki phenomenon.
slo.knowledge: By the end of this lesson, learners should be able to: (a) describe the structure of the thylakoid membrane as the site of light-dependent reactions; (b) explain the absorption of light energy by chlorophyll pigments; (c) describe the photolysis of water and identify its products; (d) explain the production of ATP (via photophosphorylation) and NADPH; (e) state that oxygen released during photolysis is the source of atmospheric O₂.
slo.skills: By the end of this lesson, learners should be able to: (a) annotate a diagram of the thylakoid membrane to show the sequence of light-dependent reactions; (b) construct a cause-and-effect chain linking light intensity → chlorophyll excitation → photolysis → ATP/NADPH production → oxygen release; (c) interpret oxygen-bubble data from Lesson 4 (Elodea experiment) using the light-dependent reaction framework.
slo.attitudes: Learners appreciate that microscopic molecular events inside a single leaf cell explain observable, everyday phenomena such as the wilting and recovery of sukuma wiki, and develop curiosity about how living systems transform energy.
slo.keyInquiry: How do plants make their own food? What factors affect the rate at which plants make food?
slo.purposeInStoryline: Lesson 5 is the first EXPLAIN lesson in the sequence. Learners have spent Lessons 1–4 gathering evidence: they predicted why sukuma wiki wilts, observed the phenomenon, tested variables that affect photosynthesis rate (light, CO₂, temperature), and counted oxygen bubbles produced by Elodea under different light intensities. Now they open the 'black box' of the light reactions to understand the molecular mechanism that converts light energy into chemical energy — explaining WHY more light produces more oxygen bubbles and WHY the shaded sukuma wiki becomes pale and grows slowly.
slo.safetyNotes: No hazardous chemicals in this lesson. If printed diagrams are used, ensure scissors are handled safely. If a torch or lamp is used for demonstration, do not direct the beam into learners' eyes. Remind learners to handle printed materials and models respectfully as shared resources.

LESSONS.overview: In this EXPLAIN lesson, learners transition from evidence-gatherer to sense-maker. The teacher anchors the lesson in the oxygen-bubble data from Lesson 4 and the unresolved puzzle of the sukuma wiki phenomenon. Using a guided annotated diagram of the thylakoid membrane, learners trace the sequential steps of the light-dependent reactions: (1) absorption of light by chlorophyll in Photosystems II and I; (2) excitation and transfer of electrons along the electron transport chain; (3) photolysis of water (2H₂O → 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ + O₂) as the electron source; (4) chemiosmotic synthesis of ATP (photophosphorylation); and (5) reduction of NADP⁺ to NADPH. Learners use a cause-and-effect chain graphic organiser to connect each step, then re-examine their Elodea bubble counts to explain the relationship between light intensity and reaction rate. The lesson closes with a DQB update, linking the molecular events to both the supporting phenomenon (oxygen bubbles) and the anchoring phenomenon (midday wilting, shade-induced pallor, overnight recovery of sukuma wiki).

framework.phase: Phase 1 — PREDICT: Activating Prior Evidence and Generating Mechanistic Predictions
framework.learnerExperience: Learners individually review their Lesson 4 data table (oxygen bubble counts at different light intensities from the Elodea experiment) for 2 minutes. They then write a 2-sentence response in their science notebooks to the prompt: 'You observed that more bubbles formed when the lamp was closer to the Elodea. What do you think is happening INSIDE the leaf cell that produces those bubbles — and where exactly do you think it happens?' Learners share their predictions with a partner (Think-Pair-Share) before 3–4 pairs share with the class. Predictions are posted on the class whiteboard under the heading 'Our Current Best Ideas' to be revisited at the end of the lesson.
framework.teacherMoves: Begin by projecting the class Elodea bubble-count data table compiled from Lesson 4. Say: 'Last lesson we showed that the more light an aquatic plant receives, the more oxygen it releases. Today we are going to open the black box — we are going to find out exactly what is happening inside the chloroplast to make that oxygen.' Distribute Lesson 4 data notebooks or display the class data. Give the written prompt verbally and write it on the board. Allow 2 minutes of SILENT individual writing — use a visible timer. After 2 minutes, say: 'Now share your prediction with your partner. You have 90 seconds.' After partner talk, cold-call 4 pairs (vary by gender and seating zone): 'Amina, what did you and your partner predict?' After each response, ask a probing question: 'What evidence from the Elodea experiment makes you think that?' Do NOT correct predictions — record all ideas on the board. Say: 'Hold these ideas. By the end of class you will be able to confirm, revise, or reject each one with evidence.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Think-Pair-Share with written anchor: Requiring learners to write before speaking ensures all learners commit to a prediction, not just confident volunteers. Posting predictions publicly creates accountability and sets up a cognitive 'return' at the end — learners must revisit and revise their own thinking, which is the core of sensemaking.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher circulates during silent writing and scans notebooks. Indicators of readiness: (a) learner references specific data (e.g., 'at 10 cm distance we got 45 bubbles per minute'); (b) learner uses mechanistic language ('something breaks down water' or 'chlorophyll captures light'). Red flag: learner writes only 'photosynthesis happens' with no mechanism — teacher makes a note to pair this learner with a stronger explainer during the diagram activity.

framework.phase: Phase 2 — OBSERVE: Guided Diagram Analysis and Annotation
framework.learnerExperience: Learners receive a printed half-page diagram showing a cross-section of a thylakoid membrane with labelled but unexplained components: Photosystem II (PSII), Photosystem I (PSI), the electron transport chain (ETC), ATP synthase, and NADP⁺ reductase. Water molecules are shown entering at PSII and oxygen is shown leaving, but the intermediate steps are blank. Learners also receive a 'reaction card set' — 6 small cards each describing one event (e.g., 'Light strikes chlorophyll in PSII, exciting electrons to a higher energy level'; 'Water is split: 2H₂O → 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ + O₂'; 'Electrons pass along the ETC, releasing energy used to pump H⁺ ions'; 'H⁺ ions flow back through ATP synthase, generating ATP'; 'Electrons reach PSI, are re-energised by light, and reduce NADP⁺ to NADPH'; 'ATP and NADPH leave the thylakoid to power the next stage'). Working in groups of 3, learners sequence the cards, match each card to a location on the diagram, and annotate the diagram with the card text. Groups have 12 minutes for this activity.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute the diagram and reaction card set. Say: 'You are not receiving notes yet — you are going to build your own understanding by sequencing these events and placing them on the diagram. This is the same thing a biochemist does when they are working out a new pathway.' Allow 12 minutes of group work. Circulate actively — visit each group at least once. Use targeted questions rather than answers: 'Where do the electrons that travel along the ETC come from? Look at your card about water.' 'Your group has ATP synthase before the ETC — does that make sense if ATP synthase needs a H⁺ gradient to work?' After 12 minutes, conduct a whole-class 'gallery correction': project the correct annotated diagram on the board step by step, one card at a time. For each step, cold-call a different group: 'Group 3 — Otieno, where did you place the photolysis card, and why?' Allow 10 seconds of WAIT TIME before accepting an answer. After each group response, ask the class: 'Does your group agree or disagree? Show thumbs.' Resolve disagreements by returning to evidence: 'What does the card say happens to the water molecules?'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Sequencing and spatial mapping (card sort + diagram annotation): Physically manipulating sequence cards activates embodied cognition — learners must justify the ORDER of events, not just recall them. Matching cards to spatial locations on a diagram builds the mental model of the thylakoid as a molecular machine, which is the conceptual core of this lesson. The gallery correction provides immediate feedback without removing learner agency.
framework.formativeAssessment: During circulation, check: (a) Do groups correctly place photolysis (water splitting) at PSII before the ETC? (b) Do groups distinguish between ATP (from ETC/chemiosmosis) and NADPH (from PSI)? (c) Can any learner explain WHY electrons must come from water? During gallery correction, count the number of groups with correct full sequences — target ≥ 75% correct after group work. Record names of groups who misplace ATP synthase or confuse PSII/PSI for targeted follow-up in Phase 3.

framework.phase: Phase 3 — EXPLAIN: Cause-and-Effect Chain and Phenomenon Connection
framework.learnerExperience: Learners receive a graphic organiser: a horizontal cause-and-effect chain with 6 blank boxes and connecting arrows. The first box is pre-filled: 'LIGHT ENERGY strikes the leaf.' The last box is pre-filled: 'ATP and NADPH are available for the next stage of photosynthesis.' Learners individually complete the 4 intermediate boxes using their annotated diagrams. They then answer 3 written questions in their notebooks: (Q1) 'In the Elodea experiment, we measured oxygen bubble production as a proxy for photosynthesis rate. Using your diagram, explain exactly where that oxygen comes from and what would happen to bubble production if we added a chemical that blocks photolysis.' (Q2) 'The sukuma wiki under the mango shade became pale and grew slowly over several weeks. Using what you now know about the light-dependent reactions, explain at the molecular level why shade would slow the plant's growth — even if water and CO₂ are available.' (Q3) 'The sukuma wiki recovers overnight even without sunlight. Does the light-dependent reaction run at night? What does this suggest about what the plant uses during the night to repair itself?'
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'You now have the annotated diagram in front of you. I want you to use it to build a cause-and-effect chain — this is a tool scientists use to show how one event triggers the next.' Distribute the graphic organiser. Give 6 minutes of SILENT individual work. After 6 minutes, say: 'Now look at the three questions on the board. These connect our molecular diagram back to the two phenomena we have been investigating all term. Write your answers in your notebook — full sentences, please.' Give 8 minutes for written responses. After 8 minutes, facilitate a class discussion for Q2 (the sukuma wiki shade question) — this is the most complex and directly ties to the driving question. Cold-call 3 learners (rotate to learners who have not yet spoken today): 'Zawadi, take us through your explanation for Q2.' After each response, ask: 'Can someone add a molecular detail Zawadi's explanation is missing?' For Q3, say: 'This is a tricky one. I am going to give you 15 seconds to think silently before anyone speaks.' [Allow 15 seconds of WAIT TIME.] Then cold-call: 'Kipchoge — what do you think?' Guide learners toward the idea that the plant uses pre-formed ATP and NADPH (or synthesised sugars from the Calvin cycle) stored during daylight for overnight repair, and that the light reactions themselves are DARK-independent only in the sense that no light reactions run — but the products are already stored.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cause-and-effect chaining + transfer questions: The graphic organiser forces learners to make the mechanistic logic explicit in their own words rather than copying notes. The three transfer questions escalate in cognitive demand — Q1 tests comprehension, Q2 requires application to the anchoring phenomenon (sukuma wiki), and Q3 requires evaluation/inference. This mirrors how scientists use mechanistic models to explain novel observations.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect notebooks at the end of Phase 3 OR use a rapid 'exit ticket' version: teacher reads Q2 aloud and asks learners to hold up 1 finger (can explain clearly), 2 fingers (partially understand), or 3 fingers (still confused). Target: ≥ 70% show 1 finger. For learners showing 3 fingers, assign a peer-tutor from the 1-finger group for a 3-minute re-explanation before the DQB phase. In notebooks, look for: (a) correct identification of photolysis as the oxygen source; (b) explicit link between reduced light → less chlorophyll excitation → less ATP/NADPH → slower Calvin cycle → slower growth/pallor; (c) acknowledgement that Q3 requires distinguishing between light reactions and the use of their products.

framework.phase: Phase 4 — DRIVING QUESTION BOARD (DQB) UPDATE: Mapping New Understanding
framework.learnerExperience: The class DQB (established in Lesson 1) is displayed at the front of the room. It contains the anchoring driving question ('Why does our sukuma wiki wilt in the midday sun even when the soil is wet — and what does a plant actually need to stay green, grow, and make its own food?') and sticky-note question clusters from previous lessons. Learners individually write on a sticky note: (a) ONE thing they can now explain about the sukuma wiki phenomenon that they could NOT explain before today's lesson; (b) ONE new question that today's lesson has raised for them. They post their notes on the DQB in two columns: 'NOW EXPLAINED' and 'NEW QUESTIONS.' The teacher reads selected notes aloud and briefly connects them to the lesson sequence: which new questions will be addressed in Lessons 6–7 (Calvin cycle / light-independent reactions).
framework.teacherMoves: Point to the DQB and say: 'When we started this unit, we looked at the sukuma wiki in Amina's kitchen garden and it raised a lot of questions we could not answer. Let's see how far we have come.' Read the driving question aloud. Say: 'Think about what you knew on Day 1 versus what you know right now. On your sticky note, write one thing you can NOW explain — be specific, use the molecular language from today. And write one new question that today's lesson has opened up for you.' Give 3 minutes of individual silent writing. Invite learners to post notes on the DQB. Read 4–5 'NOW EXPLAINED' notes aloud, affirming precise language: 'Excellent — Wanjiku wrote: the oxygen in the bubbles comes from water molecules being split at Photosystem II, not from CO₂. That is a Nobel Prize-level insight — it took scientists decades to prove that.' Read 2–3 'NEW QUESTIONS' notes and say: 'These questions about what happens to the ATP and NADPH next — those are exactly what we will investigate in Lesson 6, the Calvin cycle.' Do NOT resolve these questions — preserve productive curiosity.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Public knowledge-building on the DQB: The DQB functions as a class-wide 'working model' of understanding — a living artefact that grows across the 10-lesson sequence. Making new understanding visible and public reinforces that science is cumulative and social. The two-column structure ('NOW EXPLAINED' vs. 'NEW QUESTIONS') models scientific practice: answering one question always generates new questions.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher reads all sticky notes before the next lesson. Indicators of lesson success: 'NOW EXPLAINED' notes should include at least one of — photolysis as source of O₂, role of chlorophyll in absorbing light, ATP/NADPH as energy carriers, or connection to Elodea bubble data. 'NEW QUESTIONS' should include forward-pointing questions (e.g., 'What does ATP do next?' 'Where does the CO₂ go?') — these indicate learners understand that the light reactions are only PART of photosynthesis, which is the prerequisite understanding for Lesson 6.

framework.phase: Phase 5 — MODEL BUILDING: Cumulative Chloroplast Model Revision
framework.learnerExperience: Learners retrieve their cumulative photosynthesis model begun in Lesson 1 (a hand-drawn diagram of the chloroplast that has been revised after each lesson). In Lesson 4, their models showed the chloroplast as a 'black box' with light and water entering and oxygen and 'energy' leaving. Today, learners open the black box by adding internal thylakoid structure to their model. Using their annotated diagram from Phase 2, they add: (a) a thylakoid stack (granum) with labelled PSII and PSI; (b) arrows showing the electron flow from water → PSII → ETC → PSI → NADP⁺/NADPH; (c) ATP synthase with a curved arrow showing H⁺ flow; (d) oxygen leaving the thylakoid; (e) ATP and NADPH arrows pointing toward the stroma (labelled 'to be used in the next stage'). Learners annotate their model with a key that includes: 'INPUTS to this stage: light energy, water, ADP+Pi, NADP⁺' and 'OUTPUTS of this stage: ATP, NADPH, O₂.' They write a 2-sentence 'model update note' at the bottom: 'I added ___ to my model today because ___. This helps explain the sukuma wiki phenomenon because ___.'
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Get out your chloroplast model from Lesson 1. Look at it — you drew the chloroplast as a box. You wrote light and water going in, and oxygen and energy coming out. Today you are going to show WHAT IS HAPPENING INSIDE that box.' Display your own model (teacher's reference copy, projected) showing the Lesson 1–4 version with the thylakoid still unlabelled. Say: 'I want you to add the internal machinery we traced today. Use your annotated diagram as a reference — do not copy from a textbook, use YOUR diagram.' Give 8 minutes for model revision. Circulate and ask 3 targeted questions to individual learners: 'Show me on your model where the oxygen is produced — can you point to the exact step?' 'Your model shows ATP being made — can you write next to it why ATP is needed? What will the plant use it for?' 'Looking at your model, if chlorophyll is destroyed by a disease that bleaches leaves — like what happened to the shaded sukuma wiki — which step would stop first?' After 8 minutes, pairs do a 'model swap': each learner reviews their partner's model and writes one specific 'star' (something correct and precise) and one 'question' (something unclear or missing). Partners discuss for 2 minutes. Close by saying: 'Your model is not finished — it will never be finished, because science is never finished. In Lesson 6 we will add the Calvin cycle to the stroma section. By the end of this unit your model will explain everything we see in Amina's kitchen garden.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Iterative model revision with peer critique: The cumulative model is the learner's personal scientific artefact — it grows in explanatory power across the unit, mirroring how scientific models develop through cycles of evidence and revision. The 'star and question' peer review protocol develops scientific communication skills and ensures learners evaluate evidence claims, not just copy information. The 'model update note' requires learners to explicitly connect the model revision to the phenomenon, preventing the model from becoming an abstract exercise.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher collects models (or photographs them) at the end of the lesson. Evaluate against a 4-point rubric: (1) Thylakoid structure present with PSII and PSI labelled; (2) Electron flow correctly sequenced from water to NADPH; (3) ATP synthase correctly placed with H⁺ gradient indicated; (4) Model update note explicitly connects O₂ production or ATP/NADPH to the sukuma wiki phenomenon. Target: ≥ 80% of learners achieve 3/4 or higher. Learners scoring 1/4 are flagged for a 5-minute re-teach at the start of Lesson 6 using a simplified two-box model (PSII box and PSI box with arrows).

LESSONS.teacherReflection:
After the lesson, reflect on the following questions and record your notes before Lesson 6:
1. PHENOMENON CONNECTION: During Phase 3, when learners answered Q2 (shade → pale sukuma wiki), what percentage could articulate the molecular chain — reduced light → less chlorophyll excitation → less ATP/NADPH → slower growth? If fewer than 70% could do this, plan a 5-minute phenomenon re-anchor at the start of Lesson 6.
2. MISCONCEPTION WATCH: Did any learners claim that oxygen comes from CO₂ (a very common misconception)? If yes, plan a brief demonstration in Lesson 6 using the historical context of van Niel's experiment with purple sulfur bacteria — a powerful misconception-buster.
3. CARD SORT ENGAGEMENT: Were all group members contributing to the card sort, or were dominant learners taking over? Consider assigning roles (Reader, Placer, Challenger, Reporter) in Lesson 6 group activities.
4. DQB QUALITY: Were the 'NEW QUESTIONS' on the DQB genuinely forward-pointing toward the Calvin cycle (e.g., 'What does ATP do next?', 'Where does CO₂ enter?')? If learners are not yet asking about the next stage, the bridge to Lesson 6 (light-independent reactions) may need more scaffolding.
5. MODEL DEPTH: Did learners' model update notes connect to the sukuma wiki phenomenon with molecular specificity, or were the connections vague (e.g., 'this helps the plant grow')? Use the strongest model update notes as exemplars at the start of Lesson 6.
6. PACING: The Phase 2 card sort (12 minutes) and Phase 3 transfer questions (14 minutes) are the most time-intensive segments. If either ran over time, consider whether the gallery correction in Phase 2 can be shortened by having groups photograph their card arrangements and share one image per group rather than presenting verbally.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners reviewed Elodea oxygen-bubble count data from Lesson 4 and examined a half-labelled thylakoid membrane diagram with a set of 6 reaction-event cards describing the steps of the light-dependent reactions.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Learners traced the sequence of light-dependent reactions: light absorption by chlorophyll in PSII and PSI → excitation and transfer of electrons → photolysis of water (2H₂O → 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ + O₂) as the electron source → chemiosmotic synthesis of ATP (photophosphorylation) via ATP synthase → reduction of NADP⁺ to NADPH at PSI → release of ATP and NADPH to the stroma for use in the Calvin cycle. They connected O₂ release to the Elodea bubble observations and connected reduced ATP/NADPH production under shade to the pale, slow-growing sukuma wiki.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: More light hitting the sukuma wiki leaf means more chlorophyll molecules are excited in Photosystem II, driving faster photolysis of water, faster electron flow along the ETC, and greater production of ATP and NADPH — which is why the Elodea produces more bubbles at higher light intensities. When the sukuma wiki is moved into the shade of the mango tree, the reduced light energy means less chlorophyll excitation, slower photolysis, and therefore less ATP and NADPH produced — starving the Calvin cycle of the energy and reducing power it needs to fix CO₂ into sugars, which explains the slow growth and yellowing (pallor) of the shaded plant.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: light-dependent reactions, thylakoid membrane, chlorophyll, photolysis, ATP, NADPH, photophosphorylation, Photosystem I, Photosystem II, electron transport chain, oxygen evolution, sukuma wiki, photosynthesis rate, Elodea, chloroplast model, sensemaking, card sort, cause-and-effect chain, driving question board, Grade 10 Biology Kenya, CBE, KICD

LESSONS.title: Light-Independent Reactions: How the Calvin Cycle Builds Glucose from CO₂

slo.purpose: To enable learners to explain the light-independent reactions (Calvin cycle) of photosynthesis, including the fixation of CO₂ and the role of ATP and NADPH in producing glucose (G3P), and to connect both stages of photosynthesis in a unified flow diagram.
slo.knowledge: Learners will know that: (1) the light-independent reactions (Calvin cycle) occur in the stroma of the chloroplast; (2) CO₂ from the air is fixed by the enzyme RuBisCO onto a 5-carbon acceptor molecule (RuBP); (3) ATP and NADPH produced in the light-dependent stage are used to reduce the 3-carbon intermediate (3-PGA) to glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate (G3P); (4) G3P is used to synthesise glucose and to regenerate RuBP; (5) without sufficient light, less ATP and NADPH are available, slowing G3P and glucose production.
slo.skills: Learners will be able to: (1) construct a simplified flow diagram linking the light-dependent and light-independent reactions; (2) use evidence from the diagram to explain why shaded sukuma wiki grows more slowly; (3) interpret and annotate diagrams showing inputs and outputs of the Calvin cycle; (4) apply the concept of limiting factors to predict outcomes when ATP/NADPH supply is reduced.
slo.attitudes: Learners will appreciate the elegance of how a plant converts simple inorganic molecules — CO₂, water, and light energy — into the organic matter that feeds nearly all life, including the sukuma wiki on Kenyan dinner tables. Learners will value evidence-based reasoning when revising their earlier predictions about shade and plant growth.
slo.keyInquiry: How do plants make their own food? What factors affect the rate at which plants make food?
slo.purposeInStoryline: Lesson 6 is the EXPLAIN phase for the light-independent reactions. Having established in Lessons 4–5 that light drives the splitting of water and the production of ATP and NADPH, learners now discover what the plant does with those energy carriers. They trace the journey of a CO₂ molecule from the air through RuBP fixation to G3P and finally to glucose. By constructing a two-stage flow diagram they can, for the first time, give a complete biochemical explanation for why shaded sukuma wiki in the family's kitchen garden — though protected from wilting — gradually turns pale and grows slowly: less light → less ATP and NADPH → Calvin cycle slows → less glucose → slower growth and eventual chlorophyll breakdown.
slo.safetyNotes: No hazardous chemicals or equipment are used in this lesson. If printed card-sort materials are laminated, ensure scissors (if used to cut un-laminated sets) are handled safely. Learners should remain seated during model-building to avoid crowding. If the lesson is conducted in a laboratory, standard lab rules apply: no eating or drinking, bags stored under benches.

LESSONS.overview: In Lesson 6, learners shift from the light-dependent reactions (covered in Lessons 4–5) to the light-independent reactions — the Calvin cycle. The lesson opens with a Predict phase in which learners hypothesise what happens to the ATP and NADPH produced in the light stage, grounding their thinking in the sukuma wiki phenomenon. The Observe phase uses an annotated diagram walkthrough combined with a structured card-sort activity in which learners physically sequence the steps of the Calvin cycle using locally labelled cards (CO₂ from Nairobi air, RuBP, 3-PGA, G3P, glucose → ugali/sukuma wiki biomass). The Explain phase uses a Think-Pair-Share and guided Socratic questioning to build a two-stage flow diagram on the board collaboratively. In the DQB phase, learners revisit their posted questions and answer at least two with new evidence. The Model-Building phase asks learners to revise their personal photosynthesis models to include both stages, with arrows showing how reduced light supply in shade throttles the whole system. Formative assessment is embedded throughout via cold-calling, whiteboards, and an exit card.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners silently read a short scenario card (printed or displayed): 'Amina's sukuma wiki under the mango tree receives only 20% of the sunlight it would get in the open garden. Her friend tells her the plant cannot make enough food. Amina asks: what exactly runs out first — and why does the plant grow slowly rather than just wilting?' Learners individually write a 3-sentence prediction in their exercise books answering: (a) What do you think happens to the ATP and NADPH made in the light reactions when the plant is in shade? (b) Where in the chloroplast do you think the next stage of photosynthesis happens? (c) What molecule do you predict is the first product the plant builds toward making glucose? Learners then share predictions with a seat partner before the teacher cold-calls.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the scenario card and read it aloud expressively. Say: 'We have spent two lessons learning how light energy is captured and converted into ATP and NADPH. Today we follow that energy further into the chloroplast. Before I show you anything new, I want to know what YOU already think.' Circulate for 3 minutes while learners write — resist answering questions; respond to uncertainty with 'Write down your best guess; that is the point.' After 2 minutes of pair discussion, cold-call 3 learners (choose one high-confidence, one mid, one low based on prior lesson observations). For each response say: 'Thank you — keep that prediction. We will come back to it.' Write the three predictions verbatim on a corner of the board under the heading 'Our Predictions — Lesson 6'. Do NOT confirm or correct yet. WAIT TIME: allow 10 seconds of silence after each cold-call prompt before redirecting.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Predict-Before-Instruction (anticipatory activation): By committing a prediction to paper before instruction, learners surface and make explicit their current mental models of what ATP and NADPH are 'for'. This creates cognitive dissonance when the actual Calvin cycle mechanism is revealed, deepening encoding. Connecting predictions to the named phenomenon (Amina's shaded sukuma wiki) ensures the question feels authentic rather than abstract.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher circulates and scans written predictions. Observable indicators of prior understanding: (1) Learner correctly names ATP/NADPH as energy/hydrogen carriers for the next stage — indicates strong carry-over from Lesson 5; (2) Learner mentions 'stroma' — indicates recall of chloroplast structure from Lesson 4; (3) Learner cannot move beyond 'the plant uses energy to grow' — flags need for scaffolding during Observe phase. Teacher mentally notes 2–3 learners who need targeted questioning during the card-sort.

framework.learnerExperience: Part A — Annotated Diagram Walk (10 min): Each learner receives (or views on screen) a simplified diagram of the Calvin cycle showing: RuBP (5C) + CO₂ → 6 × 3-PGA (3C) [RuBisCO] → 6 × G3P (3C) [uses ATP + NADPH] → 1 G3P exits to make glucose / 5 G3P regenerate RuBP [uses ATP]. Key labels are blanked out. Learners use a coloured pencil to trace the path of (i) carbon atoms — in red; (ii) ATP use — in blue; (iii) NADPH use — in green. They annotate where in the chloroplast (stroma) this occurs. Part B — Card-Sort Sequencing (12 min): In groups of 3–4, learners receive a set of 10 laminated cards. Cards include: 'CO₂ enters leaf stomata above Lake Naivasha farmland', 'CO₂ fixed onto RuBP by RuBisCO enzyme', '6 molecules of 3-PGA formed', 'ATP and NADPH from light reactions arrive in stroma', '6 molecules of G3P produced', '1 G3P used to build glucose → sukuma wiki leaf cell wall and sugars', '5 G3P used to regenerate RuBP', 'RuBP ready to fix another CO₂', 'Cycle repeats 6 times to net one glucose molecule', 'Less light → less ATP/NADPH → cycle slows → less glucose → slower growth'. Groups sequence the cards in a cycle layout on their desk and sketch the arrangement in their notebooks.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute diagram sheets and say: 'You have a blank Calvin cycle diagram. Using THREE colours, I want you to trace three different stories happening at the same time: the carbon story in RED, the ATP story in BLUE, the NADPH story in GREEN. You have 8 minutes. Go.' Set a visible timer. After 5 minutes, pause the class: 'Raise your hand if you have traced where ATP is used — look at your neighbour's diagram and see if you agree on the same step.' Allow 90 seconds of paired checking. Then introduce the card-sort: 'Now your group will build the Calvin cycle as a physical sequence. Every card must be placed — there are no trick cards. The last card is your most important one: connect it back to Amina's shaded sukuma wiki.' Circulate to each group — do NOT give answers; instead prompt with: 'Where does the CO₂ come from before it enters the leaf?' and 'If this step uses ATP, what lesson told us where that ATP was made?' Cold-call 2 groups to justify placement of the card 'Less light → less ATP/NADPH → cycle slows'. WAIT TIME: 12 seconds after each group prompt.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Colour-Coded Tracing + Physical Card-Sort (Embodied Cognition + Sequential Reasoning): The colour-tracing makes abstract molecular flows visible and distinguishable, reducing cognitive overload. The physical manipulation of cards (embodied cognition) helps learners internalise the cyclic, regenerative nature of the Calvin cycle more durably than reading alone. Embedding a Kenya-specific card ('CO₂ enters leaf stomata above Lake Naivasha farmland') and the phenomenon-linked card ('less light → slower growth') anchors biochemistry in the lived context of the driving question.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks each group's card sequence for two critical placements: (1) 'ATP and NADPH from light reactions arrive in stroma' must precede 'G3P produced' — if groups place it after, this reveals a misconception that the Calvin cycle generates its own energy; (2) The last phenomenon-link card must be placed as a consequence of reduced ATP/NADPH, not as a separate standalone fact. Teacher notes groups with incorrect sequencing for targeted support in Explain phase. If >50% of groups misplace the ATP/NADPH card, teacher pauses the class for a brief whole-group clarification before proceeding.

framework.learnerExperience: Part A — Collaborative Diagram Construction (14 min): Whole class co-constructs a two-stage photosynthesis flow diagram on the board. Starting with an empty template (two large boxes labelled 'LIGHT-DEPENDENT REACTIONS — Thylakoid Membrane' and 'LIGHT-INDEPENDENT REACTIONS — Stroma'), learners are cold-called to come forward and add: inputs (light, water, CO₂, ADP+Pi, NADP⁺), outputs (O₂, ATP, NADPH, G3P, glucose), and connecting arrows labelled with molecule names. Each contributor must say one sentence explaining their addition. Part B — Think-Pair-Share Explanation (8 min): Learners answer in writing: 'Amina's sukuma wiki under the mango tree receives less light. Use the two-stage flow diagram to explain, step by step, why the plant grows more slowly. Your answer must mention: ATP, NADPH, Calvin cycle, G3P, glucose, and growth.' Pairs compare answers and refine. One pair is cold-called to read their explanation aloud.
framework.teacherMoves: Begin by saying: 'We are now going to build the complete story of photosynthesis together — both stages — on the board. This diagram should explain everything we have observed about Amina's sukuma wiki.' Point to the empty two-box template and say: 'Who can tell me the first input into the light-dependent stage?' Cold-call (do not take volunteers first) — 10 seconds WAIT TIME. As learners add components, narrate connections: 'Notice — the ATP made HERE in the thylakoid travels THERE into the stroma. The Calvin cycle does not make its own energy; it borrows energy produced by light.' Use a red marker to draw a bold arrow from Box 1 to Box 2 labelled 'ATP + NADPH' and say: 'This arrow is the bridge. If light weakens, this arrow gets thinner. What happens downstream?' Cold-call 2 learners. For the Think-Pair-Share, circulate and read written responses — prompt weaker responses with: 'You said the plant grows slowly — but WHY? What molecule is missing that the plant needs to build new cells?' After pairs share, narrate the complete causal chain on the board: Less light → fewer electrons excited → less ATP + NADPH produced → Calvin cycle cannot reduce 3-PGA to G3P efficiently → less G3P → less glucose synthesised → less energy and fewer carbon skeletons for cell growth → sukuma wiki grows slowly and eventually loses chlorophyll. WAIT TIME: 12 seconds after every cold-call prompt.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Collaborative Board Construction + Causal Chain Narration (Visible Thinking + Mechanistic Reasoning): Co-constructing the diagram makes the class's collective understanding visible and correctable in real time — errors are caught publicly and revised, modelling scientific practice. The explicit causal chain narration (using the connector 'therefore' between each step) trains learners in mechanistic explanation, a core NGSS Science and Engineering Practice (Constructing Explanations). Grounding every step in the sukuma wiki phenomenon ensures explanation remains purposeful rather than rote.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) During board construction — learner correctly places 'ATP + NADPH' as outputs of Box 1 AND inputs of Box 2 without prompting: strong understanding of inter-stage dependency; (2) Written Think-Pair-Share — learner's causal chain includes all six required terms in a logical sequence: meets learning outcome; (3) Learner conflates light reactions and Calvin cycle as happening in the same location (thylakoid): misconception flagged — teacher immediately clarifies using the diagram. Teacher collects 5 randomly selected written responses to review after class.

framework.phase: Phase 4 — DRIVING QUESTION BOARD (DQB) UPDATE (10 minutes)
framework.learnerExperience: Learners return to the class Driving Question Board. Each learner reviews the sticky notes posted in Lessons 1–5. They identify at least TWO questions on the board that they can now answer using knowledge from Lessons 4–6 (light-dependent and light-independent reactions). They write a 2–3 sentence answer on a differently coloured sticky note and attach it below the original question. They also add ONE new question prompted by today's lesson (e.g., 'If the plant makes G3P, how exactly is G3P turned into the cellulose of the sukuma wiki leaf?' or 'Does the Calvin cycle stop completely at night, or just slow down?'). Whole class briefly shares two answered questions and one new question.
framework.teacherMoves: Direct learners to the DQB: 'Look at the questions we have built up over six lessons. You now know about both stages of photosynthesis. Pick any two questions you can answer today — not questions about transport or wilting yet, those are coming. Write your answer on a GREEN sticky note and pin it below the question.' Allow 5 minutes. Then say: 'Before we leave the DQB, add one new question the Calvin cycle has raised for you — something you are still curious about. Pink sticky note, top right cluster.' After posting, cold-call 2 learners to read an answered question and their evidence. Say: 'Notice how our board is filling up with answers — we are building a scientific explanation together, piece by piece, just like researchers at KEMRI or the University of Nairobi would do.' WAIT TIME: 10 seconds when asking for new questions.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Driving Question Board Revisit (Metacognitive Monitoring + Collaborative Knowledge Building): Returning to the DQB makes learning progress visible — learners literally see their questions being answered, which builds epistemic confidence. Adding new questions models the scientific disposition that answering one question generates further inquiry. Referencing Kenyan research institutions (KEMRI, University of Nairobi) contextualises the practice as authentically scientific and locally relevant.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans green sticky-note answers for accuracy and causal depth. Observable indicators: (1) Learner answers 'Why does shade cause slow growth?' with a complete causal chain mentioning ATP/NADPH and glucose: demonstrates integration of Lessons 5–6; (2) Learner's answer is limited to 'less light = less food': indicates surface-level understanding — teacher makes a note to probe this learner during model-building; (3) Quality of new questions: questions about the biochemical fate of G3P or the role of enzymes indicate higher-order curiosity beyond curriculum floor; questions about unrelated topics indicate the learner may be confused about lesson boundaries.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners open their personal photosynthesis model (started in Lesson 3 and revised in Lessons 4–5). They now add a second chamber to their chloroplast diagram labelled 'Stroma — Calvin Cycle'. Inside this chamber they draw and label: CO₂ entering, RuBP, 3-PGA, G3P, glucose arrow exiting, and the ATP + NADPH arrows arriving from the thylakoid (their previous model). They add a 'dimmer switch' arrow from the sun to the light reactions, with a note: 'Less sunlight under mango tree → dimmer switch reduces ATP/NADPH flow → Calvin cycle produces less G3P → less glucose → slow growth.' They annotate their model with one sentence explaining the sukuma wiki phenomenon at the biochemical level. A volunteer shares their revised model under the document camera (or holds it up) for class feedback.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Open your photosynthesis model. It currently shows the light reactions. Today you are adding the second engine — the Calvin cycle — and connecting the two. Draw the stroma chamber next to your thylakoid. Use your card-sort sequence and the board diagram as references.' Allow 8 minutes of individual drawing. Circulate and check that: (1) ATP/NADPH arrows connect thylakoid to stroma correctly; (2) CO₂ is shown entering the stroma (not the thylakoid); (3) the 'dimmer switch' metaphor is present. Prompt learners who are stuck: 'What comes OUT of the light reactions that the Calvin cycle needs? Draw that first.' After 8 minutes, invite one volunteer to share under the document camera. Guide class feedback with: 'What did [name] include that strengthens the model? What one thing would make it even more accurate?' WAIT TIME: 10 seconds after the feedback prompt. Close by saying: 'Your model can now explain why sukuma wiki under a mango tree grows slowly at the molecular level. Next lesson we will use this model to explore the rate of photosynthesis and what happens when CO₂ or temperature changes.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision (Progressive Model-Based Reasoning): Requiring learners to physically add to and revise an existing model (rather than draw a new one each lesson) reinforces that scientific models are living, improving representations — a core NGSS practice (Developing and Using Models). The 'dimmer switch' metaphor scaffolds systems thinking by making the regulatory relationship between light intensity and downstream chemistry concrete and memorable. Peer feedback during the share cultivates the scientific practice of critique and revision.
framework.formativeAssessment: Exit Card (last 3 minutes): Teacher distributes a half-page card with two prompts: (1) 'Draw a simple arrow diagram showing how ATP made in the thylakoid is used in the stroma. Label the inputs and outputs of each stage.' (2) 'In one sentence, explain why Amina's sukuma wiki under the mango tree grows slowly — use the words ATP, Calvin cycle, and glucose.' Teacher collects all exit cards. Observable indicators of mastery: Prompt 1 — correct bidirectional connection between stages with ATP/NADPH labelled on the connecting arrow; Prompt 2 — causal sentence includes all three required terms in a logical sequence. Cards with missing connections or absent terms are flagged for reteaching at the start of Lesson 7.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, consider: (1) Were learners able to articulate the causal chain — less light → less ATP/NADPH → slower Calvin cycle → less glucose → slower growth — without prompting? If not, the 'bridge arrow' metaphor may need more emphasis in Lesson 7's recap. (2) Did the card-sort reveal any persistent misconception that the Calvin cycle produces its own ATP independently of the light reactions? If so, plan a 3-minute 'energy budget' mini-activity at the start of Lesson 7. (3) How many exit cards showed complete two-stage diagrams with correctly labelled connecting arrows? Set a target of ≥70% of learners achieving both exit card prompts correctly. (4) Were the DQB new questions sophisticated enough to suggest learners are thinking beyond the curriculum minimum — e.g., asking about enzyme kinetics, the fate of G3P, or night-time Calvin cycle activity? Use the highest-quality new DQB questions to motivate the limiting-factors discussion in Lesson 7. (5) Did references to Kenyan contexts (Naivasha farmland, sukuma wiki biomass, KEMRI) land as meaningful or superficial? Adjust specificity of local references for Lesson 7 if learners seemed disengaged by them.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners traced the colour-coded Calvin cycle diagram (carbon in red, ATP use in blue, NADPH use in green) and physically sequenced 10 laminated cards — including 'CO₂ fixed onto RuBP by RuBisCO enzyme', 'ATP and NADPH from light reactions arrive in stroma', and 'Less light → less ATP/NADPH → cycle slows → less glucose → slower growth' — to reconstruct the full light-independent reaction pathway in the context of Amina's shaded sukuma wiki.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Learners learned that the light-independent reactions (Calvin cycle) occur in the stroma of the chloroplast, where CO₂ is fixed onto RuBP by RuBisCO to form 3-PGA, which is then reduced to G3P using ATP and NADPH imported from the light-dependent reactions; G3P is used to synthesise glucose and to regenerate RuBP. Less light reduces ATP and NADPH production, slowing the Calvin cycle and producing less glucose, which explains why shaded sukuma wiki grows more slowly despite not wilting.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Using a collaboratively constructed two-stage flow diagram, learners explained the complete causal chain linking reduced sunlight under the mango tree to slow growth in sukuma wiki: less light → fewer electrons excited in thylakoid → less ATP and NADPH produced → Calvin cycle cannot efficiently reduce 3-PGA to G3P → less glucose synthesised → fewer carbon skeletons and less energy for cell division and growth → sukuma wiki grows slowly and eventually loses chlorophyll pigmentation.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: Calvin cycle, light-independent reactions, stroma, RuBP, RuBisCO, CO₂ fixation, 3-PGA, G3P, glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate, ATP, NADPH, glucose synthesis, chloroplast stroma, two-stage photosynthesis, limiting factors, sukuma wiki, shade effect, plant growth, photosynthesis flow diagram, card sort, model revision

LESSONS.title: Factors Affecting the Rate of Photosynthesis — Planning & Carrying Out an Investigation

slo.purpose: To plan and carry out a quantitative investigation into how light intensity affects the rate of photosynthesis, using oxygen bubble production as a measurable indicator.
slo.knowledge: Learners will know that: (1) the rate of photosynthesis can be measured indirectly by counting oxygen bubbles produced per unit time; (2) light intensity is a limiting factor — increasing it increases the rate up to a maximum; (3) other factors (CO₂ concentration, temperature) can also limit the rate; (4) a limiting factor is the factor present at the lowest/most restrictive level that controls the overall rate of a process.
slo.skills: Learners will be able to: (1) formulate a testable hypothesis about light intensity and photosynthesis rate; (2) identify and control variables (independent, dependent, controlled); (3) collect quantitative data by counting oxygen bubbles per minute at varying light intensities; (4) record data in a correctly labelled table; (5) plot a line graph of rate of photosynthesis against light intensity; (6) identify the limiting factor from graph shape and data trends.
slo.attitudes: Learners will: (1) appreciate that careful, systematic data collection leads to reliable scientific conclusions; (2) show curiosity and persistence when results do not immediately match predictions; (3) value collaborative work and fair division of roles during an investigation.
slo.keyInquiry: What factors affect the rate at which plants make food?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lesson 6, learners established that light is a raw material/energy source for photosynthesis and began to explain why the shaded sukuma wiki turned pale. Lesson 7 now moves from qualitative explanation to quantitative evidence: learners directly measure HOW MUCH light intensity matters by generating and analysing their own data. This advances the driving question — the wilting sukuma wiki recovers at night partly because metabolic demand drops, but the shade problem is fundamentally about insufficient light intensity for adequate photosynthesis. By the end of this lesson, learners will have experimental data showing a rate–intensity relationship, bringing them closer to explaining both the pallor (low light → low photosynthesis rate → less glucose, less chlorophyll synthesis) and the wilting (high light → high transpiration rate — a connection to flag for the next lesson).
slo.safetyNotes: • If using a lamp, warn learners not to touch the bulb — it becomes very hot. Keep water containers away from electrical sockets. • If using a live aquatic plant (e.g., Elodea or a locally available pond weed from Lake Victoria or a Nairobi park pond), handle specimens with care and wash hands after. • If sodium bicarbonate solution is used, avoid contact with eyes; learners should wear safety goggles. • Remind learners to report any broken glassware immediately. • If the lesson uses a simulated/printed dataset instead of live plants, no additional safety precautions are needed.

LESSONS.overview: Learners begin by revisiting the phenomenon — the sukuma wiki turning pale under shade — and predict what will happen to photosynthesis rate as light intensity changes. They then plan a fair-test investigation (identifying variables, writing a hypothesis, designing a results table) before observing: either counting oxygen bubbles from an aquatic plant at different lamp distances, or analysing a teacher-provided authentic dataset from a similar experiment. They record data, plot line graphs, and identify the concept of a limiting factor. The lesson closes with learners updating the Driving Question Board and revising their class photosynthesis model to include the rate–intensity relationship.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are shown two photographs side by side on the board (or described verbally): (A) a dense sukuma wiki plot in full Nairobi midday sun — leaves bright green and upright in the morning; (B) the same plot after two weeks under a mango tree — leaves pale yellowish-green. The teacher poses the question: 'We already know light is needed for photosynthesis. Today I want you to think more precisely — if we give the plant MORE light, does photosynthesis speed up forever, or does it hit a ceiling? Draw a quick sketch-graph: put light intensity on the x-axis and rate of photosynthesis on the y-axis. Draw the curve you predict.' Learners work individually for 3 minutes, sketch their prediction graph in their exercise books, and write a one-sentence justification below it. Pairs then compare graphs for 2 minutes and note any differences.
framework.teacherMoves:
1. Display or describe the two sukuma wiki images. Say: 'Cast your mind back to our phenomenon. The student's plants under the mango tree are pale and growing slowly. Last lesson we said light intensity is the key difference. Today we are going to be scientists — we will measure exactly how light intensity changes the rate of photosynthesis.' 
2. Say: 'Before we collect ANY data, I want your prediction. Open your books. Draw x-axis: Light Intensity. Y-axis: Rate of Photosynthesis. Sketch the shape of the curve you expect. You have 3 minutes — go.' 
3. WAIT — circulate silently for 3 minutes, noting 3–4 different graph shapes drawn by learners (flat line, straight diagonal, curve that levels off, curve that drops at high intensity). 
4. After 3 minutes, cold-call 4 learners to hold up or describe their graphs. Ask each: 'Why did you draw it that shape?' Allow 10–15 seconds of WAIT TIME after each question before accepting a response. 
5. Do NOT correct predictions yet. Say: 'Excellent — we now have four different hypotheses on the board. By the end of this lesson, our data will tell us which of these is closest to reality.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Individual Prediction Sketch-Graphs — each learner externalises their mental model before instruction. Comparing different prediction shapes creates productive cognitive dissonance and motivates the investigation. The teacher logging 3–4 different shapes on the board transforms private predictions into a public 'class hypothesis set' that the data will adjudicate.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Every learner has a sketch graph in their book with labelled axes and a written justification before the class moves on. Teacher scans books during circulation. Red flag: learners who draw no curve or cannot label axes — teacher provides a quick scaffold ('What are we changing? That goes on the x-axis. What are we measuring? That goes on the y-axis.').

framework.learnerExperience:
PART A — PLANNING (20 min): In groups of 4, learners receive an Investigation Planning Sheet. They must agree on and write: (1) Hypothesis (If light intensity increases, then the rate of photosynthesis will… because…); (2) Independent variable (light intensity — changed by moving the lamp to distances of 5 cm, 10 cm, 20 cm, 30 cm, 40 cm from the plant, OR by layering 1–4 sheets of muslin cloth over the lamp); (3) Dependent variable (number of oxygen bubbles produced per minute); (4) Controlled variables (at least 3: same plant shoot, same volume and temperature of water, same sodium bicarbonate concentration, same time interval counted); (5) Results table with correct headings and units. Groups share their draft tables with the adjacent group for peer review before data collection begins. 

PART B — DATA COLLECTION (25 min): OPTION 1 (Live plant): Each group sets up a beaker of sodium bicarbonate solution, places an aquatic plant shoot (Elodea or local pond weed) in a test tube submerged in the beaker, positions a lamp at the first distance, waits 2 minutes for the plant to equilibrate, then counts bubbles for 1 minute. Repeat for each distance (three trials per distance if time allows, otherwise one trial with class-pooled data). Record in the results table. OPTION 2 (Simulated dataset — printed card): Teacher distributes a printed authentic dataset card showing bubble counts at five light intensities from a Kenyan secondary school experiment. Learners read and record the data into their own results tables, then proceed identically to Option 1 groups. 

PART C — GRAPH PLOTTING (10 min): Learners plot a line graph on grid paper: x-axis = Light Intensity (represented as 1/distance² or simply as 'lamp distance in cm, with a note that closer = higher intensity'), y-axis = Rate of Photosynthesis (bubbles per minute). Learners draw a smooth best-fit curve (not dot-to-dot). They label the axes with units and give the graph a title.
framework.teacherMoves:
PART A: 
1. Distribute Investigation Planning Sheets. Say: 'As scientists, we never just start collecting data. We plan. Your group has 8 minutes to complete the five planning steps on this sheet. I will be checking that every group can tell me: what are you changing, what are you measuring, and what are you keeping the same.' 
2. After 5 minutes, stop the class. Cold-call one group: 'Group 3 — tell me your dependent variable and how you will measure it.' WAIT 10 seconds. If incomplete, prompt: 'How will you actually count photosynthesis happening? What can you see coming out of the plant?' 
3. Before groups begin data collection, do a 3-minute peer-review swap of results tables. Say: 'Pass your planning sheet to the group behind you. Check: does the table have headings? Units? At least five different light intensities listed? Give it back with one written comment.' 

PART B: 
4. Demonstrate the setup (or walk through the dataset card) for 3 minutes. Say: 'The bubbles you see — or the numbers on this card — represent oxygen being released. Every bubble is evidence of photosynthesis happening. More bubbles per minute = faster rate.' 
5. Circulate continuously. At each group, ask one probing question: 'Why are we using sodium bicarbonate in the water?' (Answer: to ensure CO₂ is not the limiting factor — we want to isolate light.) WAIT 10 seconds for a response before intervening. 
6. If a group finishes early: 'Calculate the average bubbles per minute across your three trials for each distance and add an Average column to your table.' 

PART C: 
7. Say: 'Now plot your graph. Remember — smooth best-fit curve, not a zigzag. Title: Rate of Photosynthesis vs Light Intensity in [aquatic plant name].' 
8. After 8 minutes, display a correctly drawn model graph on the board (without values). Ask: 'What shape is this curve? What does the flattening tell us?'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Plan-Before-You-Do Investigation Protocol — requiring learners to articulate variables and design a results table before touching equipment forces them to engage cognitively with experimental design (NGSS SEP 3: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations). The peer-review of tables builds metacognitive awareness of data quality. Connecting each bubble to the chemical equation of photosynthesis (O₂ as a product) anchors the measurement in the mechanistic understanding built in previous lessons.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) Every group's planning sheet has all five components completed before data collection begins — teacher checks this as a gate. (2) Results tables have correct headings and units (Light Intensity / cm or arbitrary units; Rate of Photosynthesis / bubbles min⁻¹). (3) Graphs have labelled axes with units, a title, and a smooth curve. Red flags: groups using ruler-and-dot graphs instead of a best-fit curve (teacher intervenes: 'In biology, we draw the trend, not every dot — draw through the middle of the points'); learners with no units on axes.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners analyse their graphs using three guiding questions printed on the board: (Q1) 'Describe the shape of your graph in one sentence.' (Q2) 'At low light intensities, what is happening to the rate as intensity increases? What does this tell you about light as a factor?' (Q3) 'At high light intensities, the curve flattens — the rate stops increasing even if we add more light. What might be limiting the rate now?' Groups discuss for 5 minutes, then the teacher introduces the formal concept of a LIMITING FACTOR: 'A limiting factor is the factor present at the lowest level that controls the rate of a process. When light is scarce, light is the limiting factor. When light is abundant, something else — CO₂ concentration or temperature — becomes the limiting factor.' Learners then complete a 'Claim–Evidence–Reasoning' (CER) frame individually: Claim — state whether light intensity affects rate of photosynthesis; Evidence — cite two specific data points from their table; Reasoning — explain using the limiting factor concept. Finally, learners explicitly connect back to the phenomenon: 'Use your graph to explain why the sukuma wiki under the mango tree grew slowly and turned pale — cite a specific region of your graph.'
framework.teacherMoves:
1. After graphs are plotted, say: 'Do not close your books. I want you to be a graph detective. Look at your curve and answer the three questions on the board in your group. You have 5 minutes.' WAIT — circulate, listening for use of the word 'increase', 'level off', 'maximum'. 
2. After 5 minutes, cold-call three different groups for Q1, Q2, Q3 respectively. For Q3, if no group mentions CO₂ or temperature, ask: 'If adding more light doesn't help after a certain point, what else might the plant need that it's running low on?' WAIT 12 seconds. 
3. Introduce the term LIMITING FACTOR using the board. Write: 'The factor present at the lowest/most restrictive level that controls the overall rate.' Say: 'Scientists in Kenya studying tea yield in Kericho use this concept — if there is enough rain but no fertiliser, fertiliser is the limiting factor for tea growth.' 
4. Say: 'Now complete the CER frame on your own — 5 minutes.' Circulate and read 3–4 CER frames. Look for: (a) claim is a statement not a question; (b) evidence has actual numbers; (c) reasoning uses 'limiting factor.' 
5. Phenomenon reconnection: 'Point to the region of your graph that represents the sukuma wiki under the mango tree. What is happening there? Now point to the region representing the sukuma wiki in full midday sun. What is happening there — and does your graph tell you anything about WHY it might wilt?' (Plant flag for Lesson 8: high light = high photosynthesis rate, but also high temperature = high transpiration — the graph cannot explain wilting alone.)
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Claim–Evidence–Reasoning (CER) Frame — this structured writing strategy (NGSS SEP 6: Constructing Explanations) requires learners to distinguish between data (evidence) and interpretation (reasoning), and to make an explicit logical connection between the two. Mapping graph regions back to the phenomenon grounds abstract graph analysis in the concrete, memorable image of the pale sukuma wiki under the mango tree, reinforcing the storyline thread.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) CER frame — Claim is a full declarative sentence; Evidence includes at least two numerical values from the data table with units; Reasoning explicitly uses the term 'limiting factor' and explains the mechanism. (2) Phenomenon connection — learner can point to or describe a specific region of the graph (low-intensity plateau region) and link it to the pale, slow-growing shaded plant. Red flag: learners who write only 'more light = more photosynthesis' without acknowledging the levelling-off — teacher prompts: 'Look at the right side of your graph — what happens there?'

framework.learnerExperience: Learners return to the class Driving Question Board. Each group receives two sticky notes of different colours. On the ORANGE note, they write one NEW question their investigation raised (e.g., 'If CO₂ is also a limiting factor, why don't farmers in Nairobi pump extra CO₂ into their greenhouses?'; 'Does temperature affect photosynthesis the same way light does?'; 'Why does the sukuma wiki wilt even though water is in the soil — our graph doesn't explain that?'). On the GREEN note, they write one question from the board that today's lesson PARTIALLY OR FULLY answered, and a one-sentence summary of the answer. Groups post both notes on the board. The teacher reads 3–4 orange notes aloud and clusters them under emerging themes: 'RATE AND LIMITING FACTORS', 'TEMPERATURE', 'WATER/WILTING'. The teacher explicitly flags the wilting cluster: 'Notice — our graph today told us a lot about light intensity and rate of photosynthesis, but it does NOT explain the wilting. That gap is what Lesson 8 is about.'
framework.teacherMoves:
