1. Say: 'You have 4 minutes — two sticky notes. Orange: one new question your experiment or graph raised. Green: one question from the board you can now answer, plus your answer in one sentence.' 
2. After posting, scan the board and read 4 orange notes aloud, asking the class: 'Is this question related to limiting factors? To wilting? To something else?' Sort them into clusters verbally. 
3. Explicitly point to any notes about wilting or water and say: 'This is our unfinished business. Our experiment today measured light intensity — but the sukuma wiki phenomenon includes wilting in wet soil. What process involves water leaving the plant? We will tackle that in our next lesson.' 
4. Update the board's ANSWERED/PARTIALLY ANSWERED column visibly — use a different colour marker to tick questions addressed today. 
5. Say: 'Look at the board. We started with one puzzling observation about a student's kitchen garden in Nairobi. Look how many questions we have now answered — and how many new, deeper questions we have generated. That is what real science looks like.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Driving Question Board — two-colour sticky note protocol distinguishes between knowledge consolidation (green) and productive uncertainty (orange). Publicly clustering new questions by theme models scientific reasoning about what is known vs. unknown, and creates explicit narrative continuity between lessons. Flagging the wilting gap sustains the overarching phenomenon as the engine of inquiry across the unit.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) Every group posts both a green and an orange note — teacher checks before moving on. (2) Green notes contain a specific answer referencing data or the limiting factor concept, not just 'we learned about photosynthesis.' (3) Orange notes are genuine questions, not statements. Red flag: groups posting vague green notes like 'light is important' — teacher prompts: 'Can you add a number from your data to make that more precise?'

framework.learnerExperience: Learners retrieve their cumulative photosynthesis model (begun in earlier lessons). They are asked to add TWO new features to the model based on today's evidence: (1) An ARROW or DIAL labelled 'Light Intensity' that can be shown at different levels — learners annotate that a higher dial setting produces more O₂ and glucose (faster rate), while a low dial setting (shade) produces less — connecting directly to the pale sukuma wiki. (2) A 'RATE CEILING' annotation or a small embedded graph sketch showing that above a certain light intensity, something else (CO₂, temperature) becomes the limiting factor. Groups then conduct a 2-minute Gallery Walk to view two other groups' models. Each group leaves one sticky note with a 'GLOW' (something the model shows clearly) and one 'GROW' (something missing or that could be clearer). Learners return to their model and make one final revision based on the feedback received. The lesson closes with the teacher asking the whole class: 'Our model is getting more and more detailed. What is it still missing to fully explain why sukuma wiki wilts at midday even when the soil is wet?' (Expected answer: the model shows photosynthesis inputs/outputs but does not yet show water leaving the leaf — transpiration. This seeds Lesson 8.)
framework.teacherMoves:
1. Say: 'Get out your class model. You are going to add two new pieces of evidence from today.' Read aloud the two tasks from the board. Give groups 8 minutes. 
2. Circulate and check that both additions are present. Prompt groups who have not added the rate ceiling: 'What did your graph look like at high light intensity? Did the rate keep going up forever? Show that on your model.' 
3. After 8 minutes, say: 'Gallery Walk — 2 minutes. Walk to two other models. Leave a GLOW note and a GROW note. Be specific.' 
4. After the gallery walk, give groups 2 minutes to read feedback and make one revision. 
5. Whole-class close: Cold-call two learners: 'Looking at our model — what process involving water is still completely missing?' WAIT 12 seconds. If no response: 'Think about what happens to water inside the leaf on a hot day — does it all stay inside?' 
6. Final statement: 'In Lesson 8, we will add the missing piece — water movement through the plant and out of the leaves. That will finally let us explain the morning wilting of the sukuma wiki.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision with Gallery Walk Feedback — iterative model building (NGSS SEP 2: Developing and Using Models) makes thinking visible and tracks conceptual growth across the lesson sequence. The Gallery Walk introduces peer critique as a scientific norm. Deliberately leaving a gap in the model (transpiration) maintains the driving question as an unresolved, generative puzzle, ensuring learners arrive at Lesson 8 with genuine intellectual need to know.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicators: (1) Every group's model now includes a light-intensity arrow/dial with annotations linking low intensity to slow rate and the pale sukuma wiki. (2) A rate-ceiling notation or embedded graph sketch is visible on the model. (3) GLOW/GROW sticky notes are specific (e.g., 'GROW: your model shows CO₂ going in but doesn't show where water goes'). (4) Exit indicator: at least 2 out of 4 cold-called learners can articulate that transpiration/water loss is the missing piece. Red flag: models that show only the photosynthesis equation without rate or limiting factor notation — teacher stays with that group during model revision.

LESSONS.teacherReflection:
After the lesson, reflect on the following: 
1. INVESTIGATION QUALITY: Did learners successfully identify all three variable types (independent, dependent, controlled) before collecting data? If not, which variable caused the most confusion — and how will you address experimental design more explicitly in future planning phases? 
2. GRAPH INTERPRETATION: Could learners describe the shape of the rate–intensity curve AND explain the levelling-off in terms of limiting factors? If learners described only the rising portion, plan a brief whole-class graph annotation activity at the start of Lesson 8 to address the plateau. 
3. PHENOMENON CONNECTION: When asked to point to the region of the graph representing the shaded sukuma wiki, could learners do so confidently? The quality of this connection reveals whether the storyline is functioning as intended or whether the phenomenon needs to be re-centred more explicitly. 
4. DQB GAP IDENTIFICATION: Did the orange sticky notes on the DQB include questions about wilting and water movement? If not, learners may not yet see the gap between what photosynthesis explains and what transpiration explains — consider opening Lesson 8 with a direct revisit of the wilting puzzle before introducing the new content. 
5. DIFFERENTIATION: Were all groups able to collect or read data and plot a graph, or did some groups spend the full period on the planning phase? If so, consider pre-completing the planning sheet for the weakest groups in Lesson 8 so they can focus on data interpretation and model building.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners counted oxygen bubbles produced by an aquatic plant (or read a simulated dataset) at five different light intensities (lamp distances: 5 cm, 10 cm, 20 cm, 30 cm, 40 cm). They recorded bubble counts per minute in a results table and plotted a line graph of rate of photosynthesis (bubbles min⁻¹) vs light intensity.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: The rate of photosynthesis increases as light intensity increases, but only up to a maximum — above that point the curve flattens, indicating that another factor (CO₂ concentration or temperature) has become the limiting factor. A LIMITING FACTOR is the factor present at the lowest level that controls the rate of a process. The pale, slow-growing sukuma wiki under the mango tree is in the low-light-intensity region of the graph where photosynthesis rate is low.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Why the sukuma wiki under the mango tree grows slowly and turns pale: the shade reduces light intensity, placing the plant in the region of the rate–intensity graph where photosynthesis rate is low. Insufficient glucose is produced for growth, and insufficient energy is available for chlorophyll synthesis, causing pallor. However, the wilting puzzle remains unexplained by this graph — water movement out of the leaf is a missing piece that will be addressed in the next lesson.

LESSONS.title: Factors Affecting Photosynthesis Rate — Data Analysis & Explain

slo.purpose: To analyse experimental data and apply the concept of limiting factors to explain how CO₂ concentration and temperature affect the rate of photosynthesis, and to connect these factors to real agricultural decisions in Kenya.
slo.knowledge: Learners will know that the rate of photosynthesis is controlled by limiting factors (light intensity, CO₂ concentration, temperature), and that at any moment the factor in shortest supply determines the overall rate.
slo.skills: Learners will be able to interpret line graphs of photosynthesis rate vs. a single variable, identify limiting factors from data, and construct written explanations linking evidence to the anchoring phenomenon.
slo.attitudes: Learners appreciate that scientific data analysis — not guesswork — underpins smart farming decisions, and value collaborative reasoning when interpreting ambiguous evidence.
slo.keyInquiry: What factors affect the rate at which plants make food?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lessons 1–7 learners built understanding of the light reactions, the Calvin cycle, and the role of water. Lesson 8 completes the evidence-gathering sequence by zooming out: now that learners know the machinery of photosynthesis, they use data to explain what 'speeds up' or 'slows down' that machinery — directly answering why the sukuma wiki under the mango tree grew pale and slowly (low light → low rate) and setting up the final two lessons on agricultural application and summative assessment.
slo.safetyNotes: No hazardous materials are used in this lesson. All data sets are printed or displayed digitally. Remind learners to handle printed graph sheets carefully and to sit safely when working in groups. If the lesson is conducted outdoors, learners should remain within the designated school compound.

LESSONS.overview: Learners open by revisiting the sukuma wiki phenomenon and their Lesson 7 graphs (light intensity vs. photosynthesis rate). They then receive two new printed datasets — one for CO₂ concentration and one for temperature — and work in groups to plot, annotate, and interpret the graphs. A structured gallery walk lets groups critique each other's reasoning. The teacher leads a whole-class Claim–Evidence–Reasoning (CER) discussion to consolidate the concept of limiting factors. Learners apply this concept to a Kenyan agricultural scenario (CO₂ enrichment in Naivasha greenhouse tomato farms) and update both their Driving Question Boards and their cumulative photosynthesis models. The lesson closes with an exit ticket that checks whether learners can identify a limiting factor from a novel data set.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are shown two blank axes on the board: (A) Rate of photosynthesis vs. CO₂ concentration, and (B) Rate of photosynthesis vs. Temperature. Working individually in their science notebooks for 3 minutes, they sketch what they think each graph will look like and write one sentence justifying each prediction. They then share predictions with a 'turn-and-talk' partner (1 minute) before three pairs are cold-called to share with the class.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the two sets of blank axes on the board or projector. Say: 'Before I give you any data, I want your brain's best prediction. Use what you already know about how photosynthesis works — the light reactions, RuBisCO, stomata — to sketch these two graphs. You have 3 minutes. Work alone and in silence.' Set a visible timer. After 3 minutes, say: 'Now share your sketch with your partner. Do you agree or disagree? You have 1 minute.' After the partner talk, cold-call exactly three pairs (choose one high-confidence, one uncertain, one who has not yet spoken this week). For each, ask: 'Walk us through your thinking — what did you draw and why?' WAIT 12 seconds after each prompt before redirecting. Record the three contrasting predictions on the board under the heading 'Our Predictions — we will test these.' Do NOT correct predictions yet.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Individual prediction sketching followed by partner comparison activates prior knowledge from Lessons 5–7 and creates cognitive dissonance when real data diverges from predictions. Recording predictions publicly creates accountability and gives learners a concrete anchor to revise later in the lesson.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Each learner has a sketch with a labelled axis and at least one written justification sentence in their notebook before group work begins. Teacher circulates during the 3-minute silent period and notes whether learners are drawing plateau curves, linear curves, or bell curves — this reveals the depth of their mechanistic understanding of enzyme kinetics and stomatal behaviour.

framework.learnerExperience: Groups of four receive two data-set cards. Dataset A: CO₂ concentration (0.01%, 0.04%, 0.08%, 0.16%, 0.32%) vs. relative rate of photosynthesis (measured in units of O₂ released per minute) at constant light and 25 °C — values show a rapid rise that levels off to a plateau. Dataset B: Temperature (5 °C, 10 °C, 15 °C, 20 °C, 25 °C, 30 °C, 35 °C, 40 °C, 45 °C) vs. relative rate of photosynthesis at constant light and 0.04% CO₂ — values show a rise peaking at ~35 °C then a sharp decline. Groups plot both graphs on provided grid paper (15 minutes), give each graph a title, label both axes with units, and annotate at least two features on each graph with a written label (e.g., 'rate levels off here — plateau', 'sharp drop — enzymes denatured?'). Groups also retrieve their Lesson 7 light-intensity graphs and place all three graphs side by side for comparison.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute data-set cards and grid paper. Say: 'You are scientists receiving new field data from a laboratory in Nairobi. Your job is to turn this data into a graph, then read the story the graph is telling. You have 15 minutes. Every group member must hold a pencil — everyone plots.' Circulate continuously. At the 5-minute mark, check that all groups have chosen appropriate scales — if a group has squashed all points into one corner, ask: 'What would happen to your graph if you spread your x-axis all the way to the edge of the paper? Try it.' At the 10-minute mark, prompt annotation: 'I can see your line — now tell me what's interesting about it. Put a star on the most surprising point and write a label.' At 14 minutes, say: 'Thirty seconds — finish your labels and lay all three graphs side by side on your desk.' Cold-call one group to describe what they notice when looking at all three graphs together. WAIT 15 seconds before taking the answer.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Data-to-graph translation (NGSS Science and Engineering Practice 4 — Analysing and Interpreting Data) ensures learners engage directly with the numbers rather than receiving a pre-drawn graph. Placing all three graphs side by side operationalises the concept of multiple variables and primes learners to see a common pattern (plateau behaviour) that points toward limiting factors.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Each group produces two correctly scaled, labelled, and annotated graphs within 15 minutes. Teacher checks for: (1) appropriate axis scales, (2) smooth curve of best fit rather than dot-to-dot lines, (3) at least two annotations per graph. Groups with only dot-to-dot lines are prompted: 'A scientist draws a line of best fit — what does that tell us that connecting dots does not?'

framework.learnerExperience: Step 1 — Gallery Walk (10 minutes): Each group posts their three graphs on the wall or board. Groups rotate around the room with sticky notes and write one 'glow' (something the graph communicates well) and one 'question' (something that puzzles them) on each other's work. Step 2 — Limiting Factor Mini-Lecture with CER (15 minutes): Teacher introduces the term 'limiting factor' using a Kenyan farming analogy, then facilitates a whole-class CER (Claim–Evidence–Reasoning) discussion. Each group constructs a written CER statement for one assigned scenario. Step 3 — Agricultural Application (10 minutes): Groups read a short scenario card about greenhouse tomato farmers in Naivasha who pipe CO₂ into their polytunnels during the day and discuss: 'Using your graphs and the concept of limiting factors, explain why adding CO₂ increases yield — and under what conditions it would NOT help.' Groups share answers; teacher consolidates.
framework.teacherMoves: GALLERY WALK: Say: 'You have 10 minutes. Move silently. Read the graphs. Leave one sticky-note glow and one sticky-note question on every group's work except your own. Be specific — write what graph feature you are referring to.' Set timer. Circulate; redirect vague notes ('great graph') by saying: 'Can you point to a specific part of their graph and explain what it tells you?' LIMITING FACTOR LECTURE (5 minutes): Say: 'Here is an analogy. Imagine a githeri recipe — you need maize and beans. If you have plenty of beans but only a little maize, the amount of githeri you can make is limited by the maize. The maize is the limiting factor. Photosynthesis is similar.' Draw a simple three-variable diagram on the board (light, CO₂, temperature each as an arrow feeding into a 'photosynthesis rate' box). Say: 'The factor present in the lowest amount relative to what the plant needs at that moment is the limiting factor.' CER DISCUSSION (10 minutes): Assign each group one of three scenarios: (A) A sukuma wiki plant in a sunny Nairobi garden at 0.04% CO₂ and 30 °C — which factor is limiting? (B) The same plant moved into deep shade — which factor is now limiting? (C) A plant in a heated greenhouse at 45 °C with high CO₂ and full light — what happens to the rate and why? Say: 'Write a CER statement: your Claim (one sentence), your Evidence (quote numbers from your graph), and your Reasoning (explain the mechanism). You have 5 minutes.' Cold-call one group per scenario. WAIT 12 seconds after asking 'Does anyone want to challenge or add to that reasoning?' NAIVASHA APPLICATION (10 minutes): Distribute scenario cards. Say: 'Read this card carefully. Use your CO₂ graph specifically — put your finger on the relevant part of the graph — and explain whether adding CO₂ will always help.' After 5 minutes of group discussion, cold-call two groups. Ask the second group: 'Do you agree with Group 1's reasoning, or do you see something they missed?' WAIT 12 seconds.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The gallery walk deploys Critique and Communication (NGSS SEP 8) so learners evaluate peers' data representations before being given vocabulary. The CER framework (NGSS SEP 6 — Constructing Explanations) scaffolds the transition from 'I see a plateau' to 'CO₂ is limiting because…'. The Naivasha agricultural application transfers the abstract concept to a real Kenyan economic context, deepening relevance and testing flexible understanding.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Each group's written CER statement includes a specific data value from their graph as evidence and names a mechanism (e.g., 'RuBisCO cannot find enough CO₂ molecules', 'enzymes denature above 40 °C') in the reasoning. Teacher collects CER sheets at the end of Phase 3 and uses a rapid 2-minute scan during Phase 4 to identify misconceptions for the next lesson. Red flag: any group whose reasoning cites only correlation ('as CO₂ goes up, rate goes up') without a mechanistic explanation.

framework.learnerExperience: Each learner receives two sticky notes. On the first (green), they write one thing they can now explain about the sukuma wiki phenomenon that they could not explain at the start of the unit. On the second (orange), they write one new question that today's lesson has raised for them. Learners post both notes on the class DQB in the appropriate columns ('What we can now explain' and 'New questions'). The teacher reads three orange questions aloud and the class votes with a show of hands on which is most pressing — this question is circled on the DQB as the class's priority for Lessons 9–10.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute sticky notes. Say: 'Our Driving Question asks why the sukuma wiki wilts in the midday sun and goes pale in the shade. Based on today's data analysis, write on your green note: one part of that phenomenon you can now explain using the concept of limiting factors. Be specific — mention a factor and a mechanism.' Allow 2 minutes of silent writing. Say: 'On your orange note, write the most burning question today's lesson has left you with. It can be about plants, farming, the data, or anything connected.' Allow 90 seconds. 'Come up and post both notes.' After posting, read three orange questions aloud and say: 'Show me with your hand — which of these three questions do you most want answered before the unit ends?' Tally votes visibly. Circle the winning question and say: 'We will make sure Lesson 9 addresses this.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The two-colour DQB update makes learners' growing understanding visible and metacognitive (they must judge what they now know vs. what remains unknown). The class vote on the priority question models how scientific communities decide what to investigate next, and gives learners genuine agency in the unit's closing direction.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Green sticky notes mention at least one specific factor (light, CO₂, or temperature) and one mechanism (e.g., 'stomata close at midday reducing CO₂ entry'). Orange sticky notes are genuinely novel — not repetitions of questions already on the DQB. Teacher scans the green notes during Phase 5 to check for persistent misconceptions before the next lesson.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners retrieve their cumulative photosynthesis model (built across Lessons 4–7) and add a new 'Limiting Factors Layer' using a different colour pen. They annotate the model to show: (1) where in the photosynthesis process each limiting factor acts (e.g., temperature affects enzyme activity in the stroma; CO₂ availability affects the Calvin cycle; light affects the light reactions); (2) an arrow labelled 'rate bottleneck' pointing to the step controlled by whichever factor is most limiting. Pairs compare models and discuss one difference between their annotations. The lesson closes with a 3-2-1 exit ticket.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Take out your cumulative model. You are going to add a new layer — use your coloured pen so it is clear what you added today. Draw an annotation for each of the three limiting factors: where does it act in the photosynthesis process, and what happens to the rate when it runs out?' Allow 7 minutes. Circulate and prompt with: 'You have CO₂ written here — can you draw an arrow showing exactly which step in the Calvin cycle is affected?' and 'If temperature rises to 45 °C, which part of your model breaks down first — the chlorophyll, the enzymes, or the membrane?' After 7 minutes, say: 'Compare your model with your partner's. Find one annotation where you drew the limiting factor acting in a different place. Discuss for 2 minutes — who has stronger evidence for their choice?' Cold-call one pair to share their disagreement and resolution. WAIT 12 seconds. EXIT TICKET (3 minutes): Project or write: 'A farmer in Kericho grows tea in cool, misty conditions (low temperature, moderate light, normal CO₂). She adds a fertiliser that boosts CO₂ release in the soil. Will this increase her photosynthesis rate significantly? Use the concept of limiting factors and refer to a specific graph from today to justify your answer.' Learners write individually on a half-sheet and hand it in as they leave.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Model revision (NGSS SEP 2 — Developing and Using Models) makes learning cumulative and visible — learners can physically see their understanding deepen across lessons by looking at the layers of annotation. The exit ticket deliberately places the limiting factor concept in a novel Kenyan context (Kericho tea, cool and misty — temperature is likely limiting, not CO₂) to assess transfer rather than recall.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator on model: At least three new annotations are added in the new colour, each correctly linking a limiting factor to a specific step in photosynthesis (light reactions or Calvin cycle). Exit ticket rubric: Full credit — correctly identifies temperature (not CO₂) as the limiting factor in cool Kericho conditions, references the temperature graph plateau or decline, and explains why adding CO₂ alone will not help when temperature is the bottleneck. Partial credit — identifies a limiting factor but does not reference graph data. No credit — states CO₂ addition will simply increase rate without considering temperature. Teacher uses exit ticket responses to group learners for targeted support in Lesson 9.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, consider the following questions: (1) Were learners able to distinguish between the plateau region (where a different factor is now limiting) and the rising region of the CO₂ and temperature graphs? If many groups annotated both graphs identically without recognising the bell-curve shape of the temperature graph, plan a brief 5-minute re-teach at the start of Lesson 9 using the enzyme denaturation concept. (2) Did the Naivasha greenhouse scenario generate genuine transfer, or did learners simply repeat the textbook definition of limiting factor? If learners struggled to specify under which conditions adding CO₂ would NOT help, consider adding a second scenario card in Lesson 9 that forces them to reason about two variables changing simultaneously. (3) Review the exit ticket responses before Lesson 9: sort them into three piles (full credit, partial, no credit). Use the no-credit pile to identify the most common misconception — likely that 'more of anything always helps'. Plan a targeted probe question for the opening of Lesson 9 to address this. (4) Did the DQB update surface any questions about topics not yet covered (e.g., mineral salts, water potential, respiration at night)? If so, note these for the unit summary in Lesson 10. (5) Reflect on group equity: did the same learners dominate the CER discussion? If yes, assign specific roles (Evidence Reader, Claim Writer, Reasoning Challenger) for the group tasks in Lesson 9 to ensure broader participation.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners plotted and annotated line graphs showing how CO₂ concentration and temperature each affect the rate of photosynthesis, and compared these with their Lesson 7 light-intensity graphs.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: The rate of photosynthesis is controlled by limiting factors — light intensity, CO₂ concentration, and temperature — and the factor in shortest supply at any moment determines the overall rate. Temperature graphs show a bell-curve shape because enzymes denature above ~40 °C.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Learners used the limiting factor concept to explain why the sukuma wiki under the mango tree grew pale and slowly (low light was the limiting factor), why greenhouse tomato farmers in Naivasha add CO₂ to boost yields (CO₂ was the limiting factor in well-lit, warm greenhouses), and why adding CO₂ to cool, misty Kericho tea farms would not significantly increase the photosynthesis rate (temperature, not CO₂, is limiting there).

LESSONS.aresKeywords: limiting factors, photosynthesis rate, CO2 concentration, temperature, light intensity, rate plateau, enzyme denaturation, Calvin cycle, data analysis, graph interpretation, CER framework, Naivasha greenhouse, sukuma wiki, Kericho tea, agricultural application, NGSS SEP 4, NGSS SEP 6, NGSS SEP 2, cumulative model, driving question board

LESSONS.title: Mineral Nutrition: What Does the Soil Give the Plant?

slo.purpose: To enable learners to identify essential mineral nutrients required by plants, describe their roles in plant growth and metabolism, and match observable deficiency symptoms to specific mineral deficiencies using KALRO secondary data and photographic evidence.
slo.knowledge: Learners will identify macronutrients (nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium, calcium, magnesium, sulfur) and micronutrients (iron, manganese, zinc, copper, boron, molybdenum), state the specific physiological roles of each mineral in plant growth, and describe the deficiency symptoms associated with key minerals including chlorosis, necrosis, stunted growth, and purpling of leaves.
slo.skills: Learners will analyse secondary data tables and photographs from KALRO field research to match deficiency symptoms to causal minerals, construct a structured comparison table of macro- and micronutrients, and draw evidence-based conclusions that connect mineral uptake through roots to the overall health and productivity of the sukuma wiki plant.
slo.attitudes: Learners will appreciate the scientific basis of soil fertility management and develop curiosity about how invisible chemical elements dissolved in soil water determine whether a plant thrives or struggles, linking this to the livelihoods of Kenyan smallholder farmers.
slo.keyInquiry: How do plants make their own food? What factors affect the rate at which plants make food?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lessons 1–8 learners established that sukuma wiki needs light for photosynthesis, water for turgor and as a raw material, CO₂ as a carbon source, and chlorophyll as the light-capturing pigment. Yet photosynthesis alone cannot explain all plant growth — the plant also needs mineral elements for building proteins, nucleic acids, chlorophyll, and cell walls. Lesson 9 closes this gap by introducing mineral nutrition as the final major input into plant nutrition, explaining why a Nakuru maize farmer whose plants show yellowing older leaves is experiencing nitrogen deficiency, and why our sukuma wiki in the shade under the mango tree gradually turned pale. Learners now hold a nearly complete picture of what the plant needs to stay green, grow, and make its own food.
slo.safetyNotes: No hazardous chemicals are used in this lesson. Learners handle printed photographs and data tables only. Remind learners that real fertiliser compounds (especially nitrogenous fertilisers) must be stored and handled carefully by adults; they are not to be tasted or handled without protective gloves in a practical setting. Wash hands after handling any soil samples if brought into class.

LESSONS.overview: Learners open by revisiting the pale, slow-growing sukuma wiki under the mango tree and the yellowing Nakuru maize field (a supporting phenomenon introduced earlier in the sequence) to generate predictions about what might be missing beyond light. They then analyse a curated set of KALRO crop-deficiency photographs and a secondary data table showing mineral functions and deficiency symptoms across common Kenyan crops. Through a structured Claim–Evidence–Reasoning (CER) activity, each group matches a deficiency photograph to a specific mineral and justifies their reasoning. The class builds a shared Mineral Nutrition Reference Table. Learners then revisit the driving question, updating their Driving Question Board (DQB) and revising their cumulative plant nutrition models to include mineral uptake via root hair cells as an additional input arrow alongside water, CO₂, and light.
LESSONS.aresKeywords: mineral nutrition, macronutrients, micronutrients, nitrogen deficiency, chlorosis, necrosis, KALRO, plant growth, soil minerals, root uptake, potassium, phosphorus, magnesium, sukuma wiki, Nakuru maize

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are shown two side-by-side images on the ARES platform: (A) healthy, deep-green sukuma wiki growing in a well-managed Kiambu kitchen garden, and (B) the same variety of sukuma wiki growing under a mango tree — pale yellowish-green leaves, thin stems, slow growth — as established in the phenomenon. A third image (C) shows a maize field in Nakuru where older lower leaves are turning uniformly yellow while younger upper leaves remain green. Learners individually write a 3-sentence prediction in their exercise books answering: 'Beyond light, what else might be missing or insufficient in images B and C? What do you think the soil provides to a plant?' They then share predictions with a shoulder partner before three pairs are cold-called to share with the class. Teacher records all distinct prediction categories on the board under the heading 'What could be missing?'
framework.teacherMoves: Display images A, B, and C and say: 'We have spent eight lessons building our understanding of what sukuma wiki needs. Light — yes. Water — yes. Carbon dioxide — yes. Chlorophyll — yes. But look carefully at image B and image C. These plants have water in the soil. They are alive. Yet something is clearly wrong. In your books, write three sentences: what do you think is missing, and what do you think soil actually provides to a plant beyond water?' Allow 3 minutes of independent silent writing. After 3 minutes say: 'Share your prediction with your shoulder partner — 90 seconds.' Then: 'I am going to cold-call three pairs. Pair one — [name student] — what did your pair predict?' After each response, probe with: 'What evidence from the image made you think that?' [WAIT TIME: 12 seconds after each probe.] Cold-call a total of 3 pairs. Record all unique ideas on the board. Do NOT confirm or deny any prediction — say: 'These are all interesting ideas. Today's evidence will help us decide which predictions are correct.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Prediction Anchor: By committing predictions to writing before seeing data, learners activate prior knowledge about plant inputs (from Lessons 1–8) and create a cognitive hook. The teacher's neutral recording of all ideas without evaluation maintains intellectual safety and ensures learners are genuinely motivated to test their ideas against the KALRO evidence.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Every learner has written at least three sentences in their book before discussion begins. Teacher circulates during the 3-minute writing period and notes (without correcting) whether learners are connecting their predictions to the phenomenon (mango shade, wet soil, yellowing) or making generic statements. If fewer than half the class mentions 'soil' or 'nutrients' unprompted, the teacher makes a brief bridging remark before Phase 2: 'Interesting — several of you mentioned the soil. Let us now look at some scientific data about what soil actually contains that plants need.'

framework.learnerExperience: Learners work in groups of four. Each group receives: (1) a printed A3 KALRO Secondary Data Table showing eight minerals (N, P, K, Ca, Mg, S, Fe, Zn), their chemical symbols, classification as macro or micro, their role in the plant, and the deficiency symptom observed in Kenyan crops; (2) a set of six laminated deficiency photographs (or ARES platform image gallery) labelled only with letters A–F showing: nitrogen-deficient maize from Nakuru (yellowing of older leaves), phosphorus-deficient bean plants from Kisii (purple-tinged leaves and stems), potassium-deficient sukuma wiki (brown leaf margins/scorching), magnesium-deficient tea from Kericho (interveinal chlorosis on older leaves), iron-deficient spinach from Nairobi urban farm (interveinal chlorosis on young leaves), and zinc-deficient maize from the Rift Valley (white striping on leaves). Groups study the photographs and data table and complete a structured CER worksheet: for each photograph, write a Claim (which mineral is deficient), Evidence (specific visual symptom observed), and Reasoning (how the data table supports the match). Groups have 18 minutes for this activity.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute materials and say: 'Your task is to become plant diagnosticians — like the scientists at KALRO in Nairobi who advise Kenyan farmers on why their crops are failing. For each photograph, use the data table to make a Claim about which mineral is deficient, cite the Evidence you can see in the photograph, and write your Reasoning — connecting what you see to what the data table says the deficiency looks like.' Circulate continuously. At the 6-minute mark, check in with one group and ask: 'You have matched photograph C to potassium — walk me through your reasoning.' [WAIT TIME: 10 seconds.] At the 12-minute mark, identify a group that has made an error (e.g., confusing magnesium and iron interveinal chlorosis) and ask: 'Both of these show interveinal chlorosis. How does the data table help you tell them apart?' [WAIT TIME: 15 seconds.] Key distinction to guide toward: magnesium deficiency appears on OLDER leaves first (magnesium is mobile in the phloem); iron deficiency appears on YOUNGER leaves first (iron is immobile). Do not give the answer — ask: 'Which leaves are affected — old or young? Now check the table again.' At 18 minutes, call time.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Claim–Evidence–Reasoning (CER) Framework: This strategy requires learners to move beyond pattern-matching to explicit scientific argumentation. By naming a claim, citing specific visual evidence, and linking it to mechanistic data in the table, learners practise the NGSS Science and Engineering Practice of Constructing Explanations. The CER structure also makes learner thinking visible and auditable for formative assessment.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Each group's CER worksheet shows six completed rows with all three columns filled. Teacher looks specifically for quality of reasoning — does the learner cite the specific symptom (e.g., 'yellowing starts on older leaves') and link it explicitly to the data table (e.g., 'the table says nitrogen deficiency causes chlorosis in older leaves first because nitrogen is remobilised from old to young leaves')? Groups that write only 'it looks yellow' without mechanistic reasoning are prompted: 'Why older leaves and not younger ones? What does the table tell you about whether this mineral moves around inside the plant?'

framework.learnerExperience: Each group shares one CER match with the class in a gallery-style debrief (2 minutes per group, 6 groups × 1 photograph each = 12 minutes). As groups present, the class co-constructs a shared Mineral Nutrition Reference Table on the board with columns: Mineral | Symbol | Macro/Micro | Role in Plant | Deficiency Symptom | Kenyan Crop Example. After all six photographs are debriated, learners individually write a 'So What?' paragraph in their books connecting mineral nutrition to the sukuma wiki phenomenon: 'Explain why the sukuma wiki under the mango tree became pale and grew slowly even though the soil was wet.' They must use at least three mineral names and the terms 'macronutrient', 'chlorophyll', and 'root uptake' in their paragraph. Finally, the class discusses how root hair cells absorb mineral ions by active transport (linking to prior knowledge of cell biology), and why mineral deficiency in the sukuma wiki is a plant nutrition problem — not just a soil problem.
framework.teacherMoves: Facilitate the gallery debrief by assigning each group a photograph. Say: 'Group 1 — you have 2 minutes to present your CER for photograph A. Tell us your claim, your evidence, and your reasoning. The rest of the class: listen and add the information to your own copy of the reference table.' After each presentation, ask the class: 'Does anyone want to challenge or add to their reasoning?' [WAIT TIME: 12 seconds.] Cold-call at least one non-presenting learner per photograph with: '[Name], do you agree with their claim? Why or why not?' After all six presentations, say: 'Now I want you to write independently for 5 minutes. Connect what you have just learned to our sukuma wiki story. Why did it turn pale under the mango tree? You must use the words macronutrient, chlorophyll, and root uptake.' After 5 minutes, cold-call 2 learners to read their paragraphs aloud. Then ask the whole class: 'We said in Lesson 3 that root hair cells absorb water by osmosis. How do you think mineral ions get into the root — is it the same process?' [WAIT TIME: 15 seconds.] Guide learners to recall that ions move by active transport (requiring energy from respiration), not osmosis, linking mineral uptake to the broader energy story of the plant.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Gallery Debrief with Co-constructed Reference Table: Distributing the explanatory work across groups means every learner hears six worked examples of CER reasoning, while the act of co-constructing the table together gives the class a shared artefact that encodes the lesson's core content. The 'So What?' paragraph forces individual sense-making and is a direct rehearsal for examination-style extended-answer questions. Connecting active transport back to cell biology (Strand 1) reinforces vertical coherence across the curriculum.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: The 'So What?' paragraph uses all three required terms correctly in context. Teacher collects or photographs 4–5 paragraphs from different ability levels during the lesson to review after class. A strong paragraph reads approximately: 'The sukuma wiki under the mango tree received less light, so it photosynthesised less. But even if light were sufficient, the plant also needs macronutrients like nitrogen to build chlorophyll, which is why leaves turned pale — this is chlorosis caused by nitrogen deficiency. Minerals are absorbed by root uptake through root hair cells by active transport. Without enough nitrogen, the plant cannot make chlorophyll or proteins, so it grows slowly.' Any paragraph that conflates 'minerals' with 'food' (i.e., says the plant 'eats minerals') is flagged for correction — minerals are not the plant's food; glucose is. This is a critical misconception to address before Lesson 10.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners return to the class Driving Question Board. Working individually, each learner writes on a sticky note one question that Lesson 9 has answered and one new question it has raised, then places them in the appropriate columns ('Answered' and 'Still Wondering'). The class reviews the board together for 5 minutes. The teacher highlights how many of the original DQB questions (posted in Lesson 1) about 'what does soil give the plant?' and 'why are leaves green?' can now be answered with precision. Learners also note which questions remain open and will be addressed in the final Lesson 10 synthesis.
framework.teacherMoves: Hand out two sticky notes per learner. Say: 'Look at our Driving Question Board. Think about what you personally now understand that you did not understand before this lesson. Write one thing Lesson 9 has answered — be specific, use the mineral names. Then write one question this lesson has raised for you or that you still cannot answer.' Allow 3 minutes of silent writing. Say: 'Place your answered note in the green column and your still-wondering note in the orange column.' After all notes are placed, read 3–4 'still wondering' notes aloud and say: 'These are exactly the kinds of questions scientists ask. Notice that [student's name] is asking whether a plant can recover from nitrogen deficiency if you add fertiliser late — that is a real question Kenyan agronomists at KALRO study.' Point to the original driving question displayed at the top of the board and ask: 'How much of this question can we now answer? What piece is still missing?' [WAIT TIME: 12 seconds.] Expected response: learners can now explain the greenness (chlorophyll, needs Mg and N), the growth (needs N, P, K), but the wilting mechanism (stomata, transpiration rate) was addressed in earlier lessons — Lesson 10 will synthesise everything.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: DQB Progress Audit: Asking learners to explicitly distinguish 'answered' from 'still wondering' questions trains metacognitive monitoring — a key competency in CBE. The public nature of the board means learners see their collective intellectual progress across the nine-lesson sequence, which builds motivation and a sense of epistemic momentum toward the final synthesis lesson.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: The 'answered' sticky note names at least one specific mineral and its role (e.g., 'nitrogen is needed to make chlorophyll, so without it leaves go yellow'). A vague note saying only 'I learned about minerals' signals shallow encoding and that learner should be targeted for additional support in Lesson 10. The teacher photographs the board at the end of the phase to track the distribution of remaining questions and use them to plan the Lesson 10 synthesis.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners retrieve their cumulative plant nutrition models begun in Lesson 1 and progressively revised through Lessons 2–8. Each learner adds a new layer to their model: (1) a soil zone below the root showing dissolved mineral ions (labelled N, P, K, Ca, Mg, S) in the soil water; (2) an arrow from soil through root hair cells into the plant labelled 'active transport of mineral ions (energy from respiration required)'; (3) annotation arrows from the mineral ions to the plant structures they support — N and Mg to the chloroplast/chlorophyll, P to the cell membrane and DNA, K to stomatal guard cells, Ca to the cell wall; (4) a revised annotation on the chloroplast noting that 'chlorophyll synthesis requires N and Mg — deficiency → chlorosis → reduced photosynthesis → pale leaves and slow growth.' Learners then write a one-sentence model caption: 'This model shows that a sukuma wiki plant needs light, water, CO₂, AND dissolved mineral ions from the soil to photosynthesise, grow, and stay healthy.' Pairs swap models and give one piece of specific feedback using the sentence frame: 'Your model clearly shows ___. You could strengthen it by adding ___.'
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Take out your plant nutrition models. You are about to make them much more powerful. By the end of today, your model must show not just where energy comes from, but what the soil contributes — and exactly where in the plant each mineral goes.' Display a partially completed annotated model on the board (ARES platform projection) that shows the soil zone but leaves all mineral labels and destination arrows blank. Say: 'Do not copy mine. Yours will be better than mine because you have just spent this lesson gathering evidence.' Allow 10 minutes for individual model revision. Circulate and look for: (a) learners who draw mineral ions entering via the stem or leaves rather than roots — correct by asking 'Where does the plant absorb water from? Minerals are dissolved in that same soil water — so where must they enter?'; (b) learners who connect all minerals only to the leaf — prompt with 'Where does phosphorus go? Check your reference table.' After 10 minutes, say: 'Swap with your partner. Give them one specific compliment and one specific suggestion using the sentence frame on the board. You have 2 minutes.' After peer feedback, ask two volunteers to display their models using the document camera or ARES upload and explain one annotation they added today.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision with Peer Feedback: The cumulative model is the most important epistemic artefact of the ten-lesson sequence. Each revision makes the model more mechanistic and more complete. By requiring learners to annotate with specific destination arrows (mineral → structure → function), the model-building phase demands that learners integrate today's content with all prior lessons. Peer feedback using a structured sentence frame ensures the feedback is specific and constructive rather than generic.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: The revised model shows at minimum (1) dissolved mineral ions in the soil zone, (2) an active transport arrow at the root, (3) at least three correctly labelled destination annotations (e.g., N → chlorophyll, K → guard cells, P → DNA/membrane). A model that shows mineral ions entering through leaves instead of roots, or that fails to connect any mineral to a specific plant structure, requires teacher follow-up before Lesson 10. The one-sentence model caption is collected as an exit artefact — it should name all four major inputs (light, water, CO₂, minerals) to confirm that learners hold an integrated view of plant nutrition.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, ask yourself the following questions to evaluate the quality of learning and prepare for the final synthesis lesson: (1) During the CER activity, were learners distinguishing between mobile and immobile minerals (i.e., did they use the 'old leaf vs. young leaf' diagnostic correctly)? If not, build a brief 5-minute review into the Lesson 10 opener. (2) Did the 'So What?' paragraphs correctly identify chlorosis as a nitrogen/magnesium deficiency symptom linked to reduced chlorophyll synthesis — or did learners conflate wilting (water/stomata issue from earlier lessons) with chlorosis (mineral deficiency)? Conflation of these two phenomena is the most common misconception in this sub-strand and must be explicitly resolved in the Lesson 10 synthesis. (3) Were learners able to connect active transport of minerals to the energy cost for the plant, linking back to respiration (Sub-Strand 2.3)? If this link was weak, note it as a preview to strengthen when respiration is taught. (4) Look at the DQB 'still wondering' notes — are any new, substantive questions present that deserve airtime in Lesson 10? Incorporate at least one student-generated question into the Lesson 10 driving question revisit. (5) Review the 4–5 collected paragraphs tonight: any learner who says 'the plant eats minerals' or 'minerals are plant food' holds a critical misconception that will undermine their understanding of the distinction between mineral nutrition and carbon nutrition — address this directly at the start of Lesson 10.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners analysed KALRO deficiency photographs and secondary data tables showing mineral deficiency symptoms in Kenyan crops including maize from Nakuru, tea from Kericho, beans from Kisii, and sukuma wiki from Nairobi.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Plants require macronutrients (N, P, K, Ca, Mg, S) and micronutrients (Fe, Zn, etc.) absorbed from soil water through root hair cells by active transport; each mineral has a specific role in plant structure and metabolism, and deficiency produces characteristic visible symptoms such as chlorosis, necrosis, stunted growth, and purpling.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: The pale yellowing of the sukuma wiki under the mango tree is partly explained by reduced photosynthesis due to low light, but is also consistent with nitrogen and magnesium deficiency causing chlorosis — insufficient mineral uptake means the plant cannot synthesise adequate chlorophyll, proteins, or new cell structures, so it grows slowly and its leaves turn pale even when soil water is available.

LESSONS.title: Model Building, Synthesis and DQB Resolution: Putting the Sukuma Wiki Story Together
LESSONS.aresKeywords: photosynthesis, leaf structure, light-dependent reactions, Calvin cycle, limiting factors, mineral nutrition, chlorophyll, stomata, transpiration, autotrophic nutrition, model building, synthesis

slo.purpose: To consolidate all learning from the sub-strand by constructing an integrated annotated model that fully explains why sukuma wiki wilts at midday and turns pale in shade, and to connect plant nutrition science to Kenyan food security.
slo.knowledge: Learners will demonstrate integrated understanding of: leaf structure and its role in photosynthesis and water movement; the two stages of photosynthesis (light-dependent and light-independent) and the molecules they produce; the four raw materials for photosynthesis; limiting factors (light intensity, CO2 concentration, temperature) and their effect on photosynthesis rate; macronutrient and micronutrient roles and deficiency symptoms; and the link between plant nutrition and agricultural productivity in Kenya.
slo.skills: Learners will synthesise information across lessons into a coherent annotated scientific model; construct cause-and-effect explanatory chains; communicate scientific reasoning orally and in writing; evaluate peer models against evidence; and write a structured evidence-based explanation using disciplinary vocabulary.
slo.attitudes: Learners will appreciate the complexity and elegance of plant biology; value collaborative knowledge-building; show confidence in using scientific evidence to explain real-world phenomena; and recognise the relevance of plant nutrition science to food security and sustainable agriculture in Kenya.
slo.keyInquiry: How do plants make their own food, and what factors determine how well they can do so in real conditions such as a Nairobi kitchen garden?
slo.purposeInStoryline: This is the final lesson of the sub-strand. Learners return to the anchoring phenomenon — the wilting and paling sukuma wiki — with a full toolkit of evidence gathered over nine lessons. They do not receive new content; instead they integrate leaf structure, photosynthesis stages, limiting factors, and mineral nutrition into one explanatory model, resolve all remaining questions on the Driving Question Board, and produce a written explanation that demonstrates mastery of the driving question.
slo.safety10otes: No hazardous materials are used in this lesson. If learners are creating poster models using scissors and adhesive, remind them to handle scissors safely. Ensure adequate ventilation if marker pens are used on large paper. No additional safety precautions are required.

LESSONS.overview: Lesson 10 is the capstone consolidation lesson for Sub-strand 2.1. The lesson opens with a rapid Predict phase in which learners revisit their original Lesson 1 predictions about the wilting sukuma wiki and note how their thinking has changed. The Observe phase uses a structured gallery walk of anchor images and data compiled from all nine previous lessons, prompting learners to identify the evidence each lesson contributed to the overall explanation. In the Explain phase, small groups construct an integrated annotated model — poster or digital — that links leaf anatomy, light-dependent reactions, light-independent reactions, limiting factors, and mineral nutrition to explain both phenomena (wilting and pallor). Groups present their models and the class uses peer feedback criteria to evaluate them. The Driving Question Board is then cleared and resolved: every remaining question is answered with evidence from the sub-strand. The lesson closes with a three-part individual written synthesis — connecting the sukuma wiki phenomenon to Kenyan agriculture and food security — which serves as the summative performance task for the sub-strand.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners open their exercise books to the prediction they wrote in Lesson 1 about why the sukuma wiki wilts and turns pale. Working individually for 3 minutes, they annotate their original prediction using a different colour pen, crossing out ideas they now know are incorrect and adding brief notes about what they now understand instead. They then share one changed idea with a shoulder partner. Two or three volunteers share with the class. The teacher writes on the board two columns: THEN WE THOUGHT and NOW WE KNOW, and begins populating them from learner contributions.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the original anchoring phenomenon photograph or description on the board. Say: 'Go back to your very first prediction from Lesson 1. Read it carefully. Using a red pen or pencil, annotate it — what were you right about? What did you not yet understand? You have three minutes working in silence.' Set a timer. After 3 minutes say: 'Turn to your partner. Tell them one idea you held in Lesson 1 that the evidence from our lessons has changed. You have 90 seconds.' After sharing, cold-call three pairs using name cards. As learners respond, populate the THEN and NOW columns on the board. Do not correct or affirm yet — say: 'We will test these NOW ideas against our models in a few minutes.' WAIT TIME: After asking each learner to share, wait a full 5 seconds before accepting the response, modelling that reflection takes time.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Elicitation of prior reasoning and metacognitive reflection — learners externalise conceptual change, making their learning trajectory visible to themselves and peers. The THEN / NOW board creates a shared record of intellectual growth that the class can reference during model building.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans annotated predictions during the 3-minute silent phase, noting which learners still hold misconceptions (e.g., attributing wilting solely to lack of soil water, or failing to link pallor to reduced photosynthesis). These learners are strategically placed in mixed groups for the model-building phase. The THEN / NOW board reveals class-wide gaps that the Explain phase must address.

framework.learnerExperience: Five stations are set up around the room, each displaying a single A3 summary card compiled from lessons 2 to 9. Station 1: an annotated leaf cross-section diagram (Lesson 2). Station 2: the word and chemical equations for photosynthesis and a list of raw materials (Lesson 3). Station 3: a photograph of the iodine-stained variegated leaf from the starch test practical (Lesson 4). Station 4: a simplified two-stage flow diagram of photosynthesis — light-dependent and light-independent stages — with key molecules labelled (Lessons 5 and 6). Station 5: a graph of photosynthesis rate against light intensity and a mineral deficiency symptom table (Lessons 7, 8, and 9). Groups of four spend 2 minutes at each station. At each station they complete one row of a Evidence Harvest table in their books with three columns: WHAT IT SHOWS, HOW IT LINKS TO WILTING, HOW IT LINKS TO PALLOR. The teacher rings a small bell or claps twice to signal rotation. Total time: approximately 10 minutes including transitions.
framework.teacherMoves: Before the gallery walk say: 'Each station holds evidence we have gathered over nine lessons. Your job is not to re-learn it — you already know it. Your job is to harvest it: ask yourself, how does this piece of evidence help explain why the sukuma wiki wilts or turns pale? Fill one row of your Evidence Harvest table at each station.' During the gallery walk, circulate and pose probing questions at each station rather than explaining: At Station 1 ask: 'Which cell layer loses water first when transpiration outpaces absorption — and where do stomata sit?' At Station 3 ask: 'What does the uncoloured section of the leaf tell us about what chlorophyll does?' At Station 5 ask: 'If a plant is nitrogen-deficient, how does that affect its chlorophyll and therefore its photosynthesis rate?' WAIT TIME: Pose each station question, step back, and wait 8 seconds before offering any scaffold — this signals that the question is genuinely open and expects learner reasoning.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Structured gallery walk with an Evidence Harvest table — learners actively retrieve and connect distributed knowledge rather than passively reviewing. The two-column analytical frame (wilting and pallor) keeps attention anchored to the driving question phenomenon throughout observation.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher reviews Evidence Harvest tables during and immediately after the gallery walk to check whether learners are making causal links (e.g., high light intensity increases transpiration rate, stomata open, water loss exceeds uptake, turgor falls, wilting occurs) rather than listing facts in isolation. Persistent surface-level responses signal the need for guided questioning during the Explain phase.

framework.learnerExperience: Groups of four receive one sheet of A2 paper (or access to a shared digital whiteboard) and the following brief: 'Build an annotated model that explains BOTH observations: (1) Why does sukuma wiki wilt at midday when the soil is wet? (2) Why does it turn pale and grow slowly in shade? Your model must include at minimum: a labelled diagram of the leaf cross-section showing stomata and vascular bundles; a summary of the light-dependent and light-independent reactions showing inputs and outputs; arrows showing how limiting factors (light, CO2, temperature) change the rate; and a section on at least TWO minerals and what happens when they are deficient. You have 12 minutes to build your model. Every member of the group must be able to explain any part of it.' After 12 minutes, groups conduct a 90-second standing presentation of their model to the adjacent group (a paired gallery format), then groups swap and repeat. After both presentations, each group receives one written sticky note of peer feedback using the sentence frame: 'Your model clearly shows... One thing that would make it stronger is...' Groups have 2 minutes to revise their model in response to the feedback.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute materials and read the brief aloud. Say: 'Your model is not a beautiful drawing — it is a scientific argument. Every arrow, every label, every word should be doing explanatory work. If you write a word and cannot say what it explains, remove it.' During the 12 minutes of model building, rotate among groups and use targeted prompts: If a group omits the stomata-transpiration link say: 'Your model explains pallor well. But walk me through exactly how the water gets from the wet soil to — and then out of — the leaf on a hot midday. Where does the balance tip?' If a group omits minerals say: 'Your model shows what light does. What does the soil contribute that light cannot replace? Think about what nitrogen and magnesium do inside the chloroplast.' If a group makes an error (e.g., stating glucose is made in the thylakoid) say: 'Interesting — where exactly does CO2 fixation happen, and what molecules from the light stage make it possible?' During presentations, stand at the back and listen. After peer feedback, say: 'You have two minutes. Use the feedback. Make your model stronger.' WAIT TIME: When a group is stuck during model building, ask a question and then physically move away for 10 seconds to prevent immediate rescue — returning only if the group is genuinely unable to proceed.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Integrated model construction — the highest-order synthesis task in the lesson. The paired gallery presentation creates authentic audience and accountability. Peer feedback using the structured sentence frame trains learners to evaluate scientific models against criteria (completeness, causal accuracy, explanatory power) rather than aesthetics. Revision in response to feedback models the iterative nature of scientific model-building.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher uses a rapid 4-point rubric (held mentally or on a clipboard) during rotation: 1 = model lists facts without causal links; 2 = model links some elements to wilting OR pallor but not both; 3 = model explains both phenomena with correct causal chains and includes limiting factors and minerals; 4 = model integrates all elements into a single coherent explanatory narrative with accurate science. Groups scoring 1 or 2 receive an additional targeted prompt before presentations. Peer feedback sticky notes are collected at the end of the lesson as secondary formative evidence.

framework.learnerExperience: The class reassembles facing the Driving Question Board, which has been displayed since Lesson 1. The teacher reads each remaining question card aloud. For each question, a volunteer — or the group whose model best addresses it — provides a one-sentence evidence-based answer. The class votes with thumbs up (fully answered), thumbs sideways (partially answered), or thumbs down (not yet answered) for each response. Questions that receive partial or thumbs-down votes are briefly discussed until a satisfactory answer is reached. Each answered question card is turned over or moved to a RESOLVED column. The driving question itself — Why does sukuma wiki wilt in the midday sun even when the soil is wet, and what does a plant actually need to stay green, grow, and make its own food? — is addressed last, answered collectively by the class in a think-pair-share before the teacher synthesises the class answer on the board.
framework.teacherMoves: Stand beside the DQB and say: 'This board has carried our questions since Lesson 1. Today we close it — but only if we can answer every question with evidence. I am going to read each question. If your group model answers it, stand up and give us one sentence — one sentence with evidence, not just a word.' Read each question card slowly. After each volunteer response, facilitate the thumb vote: 'Thumbs: is this question fully answered by science? Up, sideways, or down.' For questions that receive sideways or down votes, say: 'What is still missing from that answer? What evidence from our lessons would strengthen it?' For the driving question itself, say: 'This is the question we started with nine lessons ago. Before I say anything, I want you to think for 30 seconds — what is your best, fullest, most evidence-rich answer to this question right now?' WAIT TIME: Allow 30 full seconds of silence for the driving question think time. Then say: 'Now discuss with your partner for one minute.' Cold-call two pairs, then synthesise on the board. After writing the class synthesis answer, say: 'Amina noticed something in her kitchen garden. Nine lessons later, we can explain it completely. That is what science does.' WAIT TIME: After the synthesis, pause for 5 seconds of silence before moving on — to allow the moment of intellectual closure to register.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Public DQB resolution — learners experience genuine intellectual closure by returning to questions they themselves generated. The thumb-vote protocol distributes epistemic authority to the class rather than the teacher. Addressing the driving question last, after all sub-questions are resolved, creates a culminating sense-making moment that is earned rather than given.
framework.formativeAssessment: The quality of one-sentence evidence-based answers reveals whether learners can retrieve and apply integrated knowledge under mild social pressure. The teacher notes which question types remain difficult (e.g., questions about the Calvin cycle intermediates or specific mineral-enzyme relationships) and records these as topics for future review or homework. The thumb-vote distribution across the class provides a quick whole-class comprehension check.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners now work individually for the final 8 minutes to write a structured three-part evidence-based explanation in their exercise books. The prompt is displayed on the board: PART 1 — THE MIDDAY WILT: Explain in 3 to 4 sentences why sukuma wiki wilts at midday even when the soil is wet. Use the words: stomata, transpiration, turgor pressure, limiting factor, and light intensity. PART 2 — THE SHADE PALLOR: Explain in 3 to 4 sentences why the plant turns pale and grows slowly under the mango tree. Use the words: chlorophyll, light-dependent reactions, ATP, glucose, and nitrogen. PART 3 — THE KENYAN CONTEXT: In 2 to 3 sentences, explain how understanding plant nutrition could help a smallholder farmer in Kiambu County grow healthier sukuma wiki and contribute to food security. You may use ideas from mineral nutrition, limiting factors, or photosynthesis rate. This written explanation is the sub-strand summative performance task. Learners hand it in before leaving or photograph it if digital portfolios are used.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute or display the three-part prompt. Say: 'This is your individual explanation. Your model was your group thinking out loud. This is your thinking on paper — your evidence that you understand the story of plant nutrition from leaf to soil to food on a Kenyan table. Write in sentences. Use the vocabulary words. You have 8 minutes.' During the 8 minutes, circulate silently and read over shoulders. Do not intervene unless a learner is completely unable to start — in that case, quietly say: 'Start with what happens to water in the leaf when the sun is very strong. What are the stomata doing?' With 2 minutes remaining say: 'You should be on Part 3 now. Connect the science to the farmer.' With 30 seconds remaining say: 'Add your name and the date.' Collect written responses. Close the lesson by saying: 'Amina is still watching her sukuma wiki. Now she has your explanation. And so do you.' WAIT TIME: During the 8 minutes of individual writing, maintain a calm, quiet classroom — do not fill the silence with talk. The silence is productive thinking time.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Individual structured writing as a summative sense-making task — requiring learners to translate model-level understanding into linear prose forces precise causal reasoning and accurate vocabulary use. The three-part structure mirrors the phenomenon (wilting, pallor, agricultural application), ensuring the explanation is grounded in the anchoring context rather than abstract recall. The Kenyan context prompt connects biological science to lived experience and future citizenship, reinforcing the AERES values of relevance and responsibility.
framework.formativeAssessment: The three-part written explanation serves as the primary summative performance task for Sub-strand 2.1. The teacher uses a three-level rubric when marking: Emerging — learner describes observations without causal mechanisms or uses vocabulary incorrectly; Developing — learner provides partial causal chains for one or both phenomena with mostly correct vocabulary; Achieved — learner provides complete, accurate, causally connected explanations for both phenomena and makes a relevant, evidence-based connection to Kenyan agricultural practice. Learners who score Emerging are identified for targeted support in the following term. All learners receive written feedback within one week.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, consider the following: Did the Evidence Harvest table give learners enough structure to retrieve distributed knowledge, or did some groups simply copy labels from station cards without making causal connections — and if so, how would you revise the table prompt? Did the 12-minute model-building window allow sufficient depth, or did groups rush and produce superficial diagrams — would extending to 15 minutes and reducing the gallery walk improve quality? Which DQB questions remained most difficult to resolve, and what does this reveal about which lessons in the sequence need strengthening in future delivery? Did the three-part written task successfully discriminate between learners who understood at a surface level and those who understood causally — and were the vocabulary constraints helpful or limiting? Finally, consider whether the Kenyan agricultural context in Part 3 felt genuine and meaningful to learners, or whether a more specific local scenario (e.g., a named county extension officer, a KALRO recommendation, or a specific cooperative) would make the connection more authentic next time.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: We revisited the original sukuma wiki wilting and pallor phenomenon and reviewed evidence gathered across nine lessons through annotated predictions, an Evidence Harvest gallery walk, and group annotated models integrating leaf structure, photosynthesis stages, limiting factors, and mineral nutrition.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: The wilting of sukuma wiki at midday — even when soil is wet — occurs because high light intensity and temperature accelerate transpiration through open stomata faster than roots can absorb and transport water upward through xylem, causing loss of turgor pressure in leaf cells; this is a reversible process that reverses at night when transpiration slows and turgor is restored. The pallor and slow growth in shade occur because reduced light intensity limits the light-dependent reactions, reducing ATP and NADPH production, which in turn limits the Calvin cycle output of glucose; with less glucose available for growth and less chlorophyll synthesised (especially if nitrogen or magnesium are also limiting), the plant grows slowly and appears pale yellow-green.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: By integrating leaf anatomy, the two-stage mechanism of photosynthesis, the concept of limiting factors, and the roles of soil minerals, we can fully resolve the driving question: the sukuma wiki plant needs light as an energy source for the light-dependent reactions, water and CO2 as raw materials for both stages, chlorophyll to absorb light, and minerals such as nitrogen and magnesium from the soil to build chlorophyll and enzymes — when any of these factors is limiting, the plant cannot make food efficiently, leading to the observable symptoms Amina saw in her Nairobi kitchen garden; understanding these mechanisms helps Kenyan smallholder farmers make evidence-based decisions about irrigation timing, shade management, and fertiliser application to improve sukuma wiki yield and contribute to household and national food security.

FINAL_EXPLANATION.instructions: This is your Final Explanation document. Your task is to write a thorough, well-organised answer to the driving question using everything you have learned across the 10 lessons in this unit. Read the driving question carefully, then use each section below as a guide. For every claim you make, support it with evidence from your observations, experiments, or readings. A strong Final Explanation connects ideas across sections — show how wilting, photosynthesis, chlorophyll, and mineral nutrition are all linked in the life of your sukuma wiki plant. Write in full sentences and use correct scientific vocabulary throughout.

sections.title: Section 1: Why Does the Sukuma Wiki Wilt at Midday — Even When the Soil Is Wet?
sections.prompt:
Explain what is happening inside the sukuma wiki leaves during the hottest part of the day. In your answer:
• Describe what transpiration is and where it occurs in the leaf.
• Explain why transpiration rates are highest at midday (think about temperature, sunlight intensity, and stomata).
• Explain why wilting occurs even when the soil is moist — what does this tell us about the RATE of water loss versus water uptake?
• Use evidence from your investigations (e.g., the Vaseline-leaf activity or the potometer data) to support your explanation.
• Explain why the plant recovers overnight — what changes between midday and the following morning?
sections.exemplar: During the midday heat in Nairobi, the sukuma wiki wilts because it is losing water through transpiration faster than its roots can absorb water from the soil. Transpiration is the evaporation of water vapour from the leaves, mainly through tiny pores called stomata on the lower surface of the leaf. At midday, high temperatures increase the kinetic energy of water molecules inside the leaf, causing them to evaporate rapidly. At the same time, bright sunlight causes the stomata to open wide to allow carbon dioxide in for photosynthesis. With stomata wide open and temperatures high, the rate of transpiration peaks. Even though the soil is moist, the roots cannot absorb and deliver water up the stem quickly enough to replace what is being lost. This creates a water deficit in the leaf cells — the cells lose turgor pressure and become flaccid, causing the leaf to wilt. In our Vaseline experiment, leaves coated on the underside (where most stomata are) wilted much more slowly than untreated leaves, confirming that stomata are the main exit point for water vapour. Overnight, temperatures drop, sunlight disappears, and the stomata close. Transpiration almost stops, allowing the roots to slowly reabsorb water from the moist soil, refilling the leaf cells and restoring turgor pressure. That is why the plant looks healthy again each morning — the damage is reversible because the soil still contains water.

sections.title: Section 2: What Is Photosynthesis, and Why Does the Plant Need Sunlight?
sections.prompt:
Explain the process of photosynthesis in clear detail. In your answer:
• State the raw materials (inputs) and the products (outputs) of photosynthesis.
• Write the word equation and, if you can, the balanced chemical equation.
• Explain the role of sunlight — why can't the plant make food without it?
• Explain where in the cell photosynthesis takes place and why.
• Use your observations from the variegated leaf starch test or the light-intensity investigation to support your explanation.
sections.exemplar: Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants use light energy to convert carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen. The word equation is: Carbon dioxide + Water → Glucose + Oxygen. The balanced chemical equation is: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Sunlight provides the energy needed to break apart water molecules and to power the chemical reactions that build glucose from carbon dioxide. Without light, these reactions cannot proceed — the plant has no energy source to drive them. Photosynthesis takes place in the chloroplasts, which are found mainly in the mesophyll cells of the leaf. Chloroplasts contain a green pigment called chlorophyll, which absorbs light energy — particularly red and blue wavelengths — and uses it to drive photosynthesis. In our variegated leaf experiment, only the green parts of the leaf (which contained chlorophyll) turned blue-black when tested with iodine solution, proving that starch was only produced where chlorophyll was present and light was absorbed. In our light-intensity investigation, we found that as we moved the lamp closer to the Elodea water plant — increasing light intensity — the rate of oxygen bubble production increased, showing that more photosynthesis was occurring. This directly explains why the sukuma wiki under the mango tree grows more slowly: the shade reduces light intensity, so the plant photosynthesises less, produces less glucose, and therefore has less energy and materials for growth.

sections.title: Section 3: Why Do the Leaves Turn Pale Yellow-Green in the Shade?
sections.prompt:
Explain why the sukuma wiki leaves gradually become pale yellowish-green when the plant is moved under the mango tree. In your answer:
• Explain what chlorophyll is and what it does.
• Explain how light affects chlorophyll production in the leaf.
• Connect the loss of chlorophyll to the reduced rate of photosynthesis and slower growth.
• Mention any other factors (such as mineral nutrients) that could also cause yellowing, and explain the evidence for this from your investigations.
sections.exemplar: The green colour of sukuma wiki leaves comes from chlorophyll, a pigment found inside chloroplasts that absorbs light energy to power photosynthesis. When the plant is moved into heavy shade under the mango tree, it receives very little light.
